BOX19.blurb:
The men's 55kg final headlines an evening session that also delivers three semifinals — a rare chance to watch gold won and gold pursued in the same two hours. The compact card keeps the energy tight and the stakes unmistakable.

This is the venue boxing fans dream about: 3,600 seats wrapped around a single ring, no bad sightlines, and a crowd that can swing momentum with a roar. Every punch echoes off the downtown LA walls.

The 55kg division falls in the bantamweight range, historically a technical showcase for speed and footwork. If Keyshawn Davis qualifies and drops weight, the 55kg or 60kg classes could bring American medal hopes to fever pitch.
BOX19.potentialContendersIntro: Names depend on World Boxing qualification, but these athletes could shape the session if they compete.

potentialContenders.name: Keyshawn Davis
potentialContenders.note: Tokyo pro competitor; potential contender if eligibility allows and weight aligns

BOX20.blurb:
The men's super heavyweight final anchors a midday session that also features semifinals at 60kg for both men and women. Gold at stake in the ring's biggest division — over 90kg, where every punch carries knockout power and fighters can end bouts in seconds.

DTLA Arena's intimacy means you'll hear the impact when a super heavyweight lands clean. The crowd noise in a 3,600-seat room for a gold medal bout is deafening, and you're close enough to see corner advice between rounds.

Bakhodir Jalolov of Uzbekistan won Paris gold in this division at 6'7 with devastating power. If he returns to defend, the final could become one of the Games' most anticipated heavyweight clashes.
BOX20.potentialContendersIntro: Projected contenders for the men's +90kg final, based on recent Olympic and world championship results.

potentialContenders.name: Bakhodir Jalolov
potentialContenders.note: Paris 2024 gold medalist; reigning super heavyweight champion if he qualifies

BOX21.blurb:
Women's 51kg crowns its Olympic champion in an evening session that also stages semifinals at 75kg and men's 65kg. The 51kg final — the lightest women's class — is a study in speed, precision, and relentless conditioning over three two-minute rounds.

The DTLA Arena setting makes every exchange feel immediate. In a room this small, you can track head movement, hear leather hit flesh, and watch corner teams react in real time as strategies shift.

LA28's expanded women's program means 51kg is one of seven weight classes, each drawing global depth. The gold medalist will come from a field shaped by World Boxing's new qualification pathway, likely featuring Paris contenders and rising challengers from underrepresented regions.

BOX22.blurb:
Two gold medals decided in ninety minutes — women's 65kg and men's 90kg — plus a semifinal at women's 57kg. The 65kg final could be a marquee bout if Imane Khelif qualifies after her Paris gold at 66kg, making this session a potential showcase for the sport's highest-profile athlete.

DTLA Arena's compact design creates an atmosphere more fight club than stadium. You're close to the action, and the crowd's energy is immediate and visceral, especially when a home contender or global star steps through the ropes.

The men's 90kg division sits at the upper weight threshold before super heavyweight, producing technical fighters with real power. Expect crisp combinations and knockout threat in every exchange as two finalists battle for gold.
BOX22.potentialContendersIntro: Potential contenders for the women's 65kg final, pending World Boxing qualification.

potentialContenders.name: Imane Khelif
potentialContenders.note: Paris 2024 gold at 66kg; likely to compete at 65kg or 70kg if she qualifies for LA28

BOX23.blurb:
Four gold medal bouts in one evening — the densest finals session of the boxing program. Women's 54kg and 60kg, men's 60kg and 80kg, all decided back-to-back in a compact two-hour window that will crown champions in rapid succession.

This is Olympic boxing at its most concentrated: DTLA Arena's 3,600 seats, no intermission between bouts, and the pressure of four national anthems waiting to be played. The crowd stays hot because there's no time to cool down.

The 60kg classes — lightweight for men, a mid-range technical division for women — often produce the Games' most skilled matchups, balancing speed and power. Expect tactical brilliance and late-round drama as fighters chase gold in the final seconds.

BOX24.blurb:
The final session of LA28 boxing: four gold medal bouts closing out the sport's Olympic chapter. Women's 57kg and 75kg, men's 65kg and 70kg — the last punches thrown, the last champions crowned, the last national flags raised in the DTLA Arena ring.

The atmosphere will be bittersweet and electric, a small room packed with fans who know they're witnessing the end. Every bout carries the weight of finality, and the crowd noise will peak as the last gold medalist is announced.

Keyshawn Davis could be a factor at men's 65kg if pro eligibility allows and he qualifies — a potential American gold in front of a home crowd to close the tournament. The women's 75kg final showcases the depth of LA28's historic gender parity, seven weight classes that have reshaped the Olympic boxing landscape.
BOX24.potentialContendersIntro: Projected contenders for the men's 65kg final, based on recent form and pro eligibility speculation.

potentialContenders.name: Keyshawn Davis
potentialContenders.note: Tokyo pro competitor; could compete at 65kg welterweight if rules allow and he qualifies

BSB01.blurb:
Baseball returns to the Olympics after a 20-year absence, and the first pitch will be thrown at Dodger Stadium — the same venue that hosted the sport as a demonstration event in 1984. This opening round matchup features two of the six qualifying nations in a round-robin format that sets the stage for the knockout rounds.

MLB and the Players Association are still negotiating whether active major leaguers can participate, which would transform this from a quality international tournament into a once-in-a-generation showcase. If an agreement is reached, you could see big league stars representing their countries at baseball's most iconic West Coast venue.

Dodger Stadium in mid-July — palm trees beyond the outfield pavilions, the San Gabriel Mountains visible from the upper deck, and a sport coming home to the Olympics. Morning first pitch means you'll catch the game in full sun before the marine layer rolls in.
BSB01.potentialContendersIntro: USA automatically qualified as host. Dominican Republic and Venezuela have already punched their tickets through the 2026 World Baseball Classic, with Japan projected to claim one of the remaining spots.

BSB02.blurb:
The first night game of the Olympic baseball tournament lights up Dodger Stadium under the iconic backdrop of downtown LA. Two nations square off in pool play, each chasing a spot in the quarterfinals.

This session could feature a marquee international rivalry — USA vs. Dominican Republic or Japan vs. Venezuela — depending on how the schedule shakes out. If MLB's player release comes through, the evening atmosphere will carry big league intensity with Olympic stakes. If not, you'll still see elite international rosters built around Nippon Professional Baseball stars and top minor leaguers.

Sunset in Chavez Ravine is one of baseball's great visual moments. The stadium glows as the sky shifts from blue to purple behind the outfield, and 56,000 fans pack the terraces for a game that actually matters for a medal.
BSB02.potentialContendersIntro: USA has a projected roster anchored by Adley Rutschman, Aaron Judge, and Bryce Harper — if MLB allows it. Japan's NPB is expected to release players, making them a lock for elite talent regardless of MLB's decision.

BSB03.blurb:
Pool play continues with another daytime doubleheader slate. Teams are battling for seeding heading into the single-elimination quarterfinals — every win matters in a six-team round-robin.

The stakes escalate with each game. A loss here doesn't eliminate you, but it makes the path to a medal significantly harder. Expect pitching strategy to come into focus as managers balance winning today with saving arms for the knockout rounds that start in two days.

Dodger Stadium's field-level seats put you close enough to hear the chatter between dugouts and the crack of the bat off a wooden barrel. The sun is high and the sightlines are clean — classic baseball in an Olympic setting.
BSB03.potentialContendersIntro: The field depends on 2027 Premier12 results and a March 2028 qualifier, but Japan, USA, Dominican Republic, and Venezuela are the strongest projected contenders based on current international form.

BSB04.blurb:
The final weekend of pool play wraps up under the lights at Dodger Stadium. By now the tournament pecking order is starting to reveal itself — who's got the pitching depth, who's clutch in tight games, which lineup is dangerous top to bottom.

This is the last chance to fine-tune rosters and positioning before the single-elimination rounds begin Monday. Managers may use this session to get a look at relievers or give at-bats to bench players, or they may go all-in if seeding is still on the line. The uncertainty makes it compelling.

A Saturday night crowd in Dodger Stadium is a baseball pilgrimage. The Pavilion sections bring the noise, and the organ echoes through the bowl between innings. You're watching Olympic baseball in the venue that helped invent the modern ballpark experience.
BSB04.potentialContendersIntro: Japan's Yoshinobu Yamamoto could pitch for his home country at the stadium where he plays for the Dodgers. USA's projected pitching staff includes flamethrower Paul Skenes if MLB allows.

BSB05.blurb:
The final day of round-robin play determines seeding for Monday's quarterfinals. A team that looked shaky early in the week could punch its ticket to the medal rounds with a strong finish here.

This is where roster construction shows itself — which pitching staffs still have gas in the tank, which lineups have enough depth to sustain offensive production across six straight days of games. International baseball rewards versatility and endurance as much as star power.

Dodger Stadium on a Sunday morning is a Los Angeles tradition. The parking lots open early, the sun warms the bleachers, and you're settling in for meaningful Olympic baseball before most of the city wakes up.
BSB05.potentialContendersIntro: USA features projected stars like Adley Rutschman behind the plate and Triston Casas at first base if MLB reaches an agreement. Japan's NPB stars are expected regardless.

BSB06.blurb:
Pool play concludes with the final round-robin game under the lights. By the time this session ends, the quarterfinal bracket will be set and the medal chase begins in earnest the next day.

Teams may rest key starters if they've already locked in seeding, or they may need every inning to avoid an early exit. The format is unforgiving — finish outside the top four and you're on a plane home without playing another game.

Dodger Stadium's night atmosphere is electric when the stakes are real. The stadium glows against the LA skyline, the crowd gets loud on two-strike counts, and baseball under the lights has a rhythm you don't get in daytime sessions.
BSB06.potentialContendersIntro: MLB Commissioner Rob Manfred has expressed optimism about an agreement that would allow stars like Aaron Judge and Shohei Ohtani to compete. No deal is finalized, but the groundwork is being laid.

BSB07.blurb:
Win or go home. The quarterfinals begin with a noon first pitch, and the losing team's Olympic tournament ends before the day does.

Single-elimination baseball magnifies every decision — bullpen management, defensive positioning, pinch-hit timing. There's no tomorrow to adjust or regroup. This is the round where tournament baseball separates from the regular season grind — the best pitcher may throw on short rest, the closer may come in for six outs, and the bench gets emptied in the late innings.

The energy in Dodger Stadium shifts when elimination is on the line. Every pitch carries weight, and the crowd reacts accordingly. You're watching players chase a medal, not a playoff series — the urgency is different.
BSB07.potentialContendersIntro: USA, Dominican Republic, and Venezuela are locked in. Japan is projected to qualify through 2027 Premier12. Quarterfinal matchups won't be known until pool play concludes July 16.

BSB08.blurb:
The second quarterfinal of the night session delivers another do-or-die matchup. One team advances to the semifinals with a medal within reach; the other watches the rest of the tournament from the stands.

Expect aggressive managing — stolen bases in scoring position, defensive shifts on every batter, relief aces entering in the seventh inning. International tournament baseball rewards calculated risk-taking, and managers know they won't get another chance if they hold back.

Night baseball at Dodger Stadium has a voltage you can feel from the bleachers. The white of the uniforms pops under the lights, the sound of the ball hitting the glove echoes crisper, and 56,000 fans pack the bowl for a game with Olympic stakes.
BSB08.potentialContendersIntro: The quarterfinal bracket depends on pool play results. Dominican Republic is confirmed; Puerto Rico, Venezuela, or Canada will claim the second Americas spot based on WBC outcomes.

BSB09.blurb:
Four teams remain. Two will play for gold on Wednesday night; two will play for bronze in the afternoon. This semifinal determines who gets which shot.

The pressure ratchets up another level — pitchers are working on fumes after six straight days of games, benches are thin, and every defensive play feels magnified. One error, one hanging slider, one missed sign can end a medal dream. The margin for error has evaporated.

Dodger Stadium's midday shadows creep across the infield in July, creating a brutal backdrop for hitters. The pitcher releases from sunlight and the ball arrives from shade — an advantage that rewards command and deception.
BSB09.potentialContendersIntro: USA auto-qualified and Dominican Republic secured their spots through the 2026 WBC. Japan is projected to qualify and would bring Shohei Ohtani if MLB allows player participation.

BSB10.blurb:
The second semifinal of the evening decides the final two medal-round participants. Win and you're guaranteed at minimum a bronze; lose and you go home empty.

This is where international baseball greatness gets defined. A clutch home run here echoes for a generation. A shutdown pitching performance becomes part of Olympic lore. The players know the stakes — this is their one chance to win a medal for their country on the sport's biggest global stage.

Dodger Stadium under the lights for an Olympic semifinal — the pavilions are deafening, the organist plays between innings, and you're watching baseball history unfold in real time. This is what the sport looks like when everything is on the line.
BSB10.potentialContendersIntro: If MLB allows participation, this semifinal could feature Ohtani for Japan, Soto for the Dominican Republic, Judge for USA, or Acuna for Venezuela — the sport's biggest names on the Olympic stage.

BSB11.blurb:
The bronze medal game is a bucket-list event even without gold on the line. Two nations that fell one game short of the final now battle for a spot on the podium — and Olympic bronze in baseball is still hardware few players ever touch.

This session rewards the team that handles disappointment better. Both squads just lost heartbreakers in the semifinals 18 hours ago. The ability to reset, refocus, and summon one more competitive performance separates medalists from also-rans.

Dodger Stadium at noon on the final day of the tournament — the venue has hosted seven straight days of Olympic baseball, and the bronze medal game delivers one last chance to see elite international competition at one of the sport's cathedral venues.
BSB11.potentialContendersIntro: The bronze medal game will feature two of the four semifinalists: USA (host), Dominican Republic and Venezuela (WBC qualifiers), and likely Japan (projected Premier12 qualifier). If MLB allows players, this could still feature megastars.

BSB12.blurb:
Baseball's gold medal game at Dodger Stadium. If MLB releases its players, this is Ohtani, Judge, Soto, and Acuna — the best players on earth — competing for their countries under the Olympic rings. If not, it's still elite international baseball with a gold medal on the line.

The entire tournament has built to this. Nine innings, winner takes gold, and the final out will be replayed forever. This is the session that could produce the signature moment of LA28 — a walk-off home run, a complete-game shutout, a defensive gem in the ninth that saves a one-run lead.

Dodger Stadium at 10 p.m. on the final night of the baseball tournament — the energy is seismic, the stakes are absolute, and you're watching Olympic history in a venue that helped define American sports architecture. This is what baseball looks like when everything is on the line.
BSB12.potentialContendersIntro: The gold medal game will feature two of these projected qualifiers: USA, Japan, Dominican Republic, or Venezuela. If MLB allows participation, this could be the greatest collection of baseball talent ever assembled for a single game.

BSB13.blurb:
The Olympic softball tournament opens in Oklahoma City with morning round-robin play. Six nations begin their medal campaigns in a full slate of preliminary games.

Team USA enters as the automatic qualifier and historical powerhouse, seeking redemption after Japan's upset gold in Tokyo. Each match matters in determining bracket seeding for the knockout rounds ahead.

OKC Softball Park is the premier softball-specific venue in America, home to the Women's College World Series. Expect a knowledgeable crowd and intimate sightlines for the sport's Olympic return.
BSB13.potentialContendersIntro: Team USA and defending champion Japan are projected as the top contenders, with qualification still underway for the remaining four spots.

potentialContenders.name: USA
potentialContenders.note: Automatic qualifier as host nation; most decorated Olympic softball program with four golds

potentialContenders.name: Japan
potentialContenders.note: Defending Olympic champion from Tokyo 2020 gold medal upset over USA

BSB14.blurb:
The afternoon session brings a second wave of round-robin matchups as teams settle into Olympic rhythm. With only six nations competing, every preliminary game carries weight.

The format rewards consistency — top finishers earn advantageous semifinal placement. Expect competitive at-bats as programs jockey for position.

OKC Softball Park sits in the heart of American softball country. The 13,000-seat venue hosts the sport's biggest collegiate stage annually; now it gets the Olympic spotlight.
BSB14.potentialContendersIntro: USA and Japan are expected to headline the field, with emerging NCAA stars potentially suiting up for Team USA.

potentialContenders.name: USA
potentialContenders.note: Confirmed qualifier; deep pool includes prospects like Mia Williams and NiJaree Canady from Texas Tech

BSB15.blurb:
Prime-time softball under the Oklahoma lights closes the opening day slate. The evening session brings three more preliminary round games in the six-team tournament.

This is softball's first Olympic appearance since Tokyo 2020, and OKC is its American home base. The venue hosts USA Softball's headquarters and national team training.

Night games at Devon Park carry a festival atmosphere — fans pack the concourses, and the outfield dimensions feel intimate from every seat. The sport's Olympic homecoming gets a proper stage.
BSB15.potentialContendersIntro: Team USA enters with home advantage and a roster likely featuring top NCAA talent, while Japan returns as the defending champions.

BSB16.blurb:
Day two of the round-robin opens with morning matchups as the preliminary standings begin to take shape. Teams adjust strategies after opening-day results.

With a full round-robin format, every team faces every opponent once before the medal rounds. Strong pitching rotations become crucial across the five-day preliminary window.

OKC Softball Park doubles as the Women's College World Series home, and the venue knows how to stage high-stakes softball. The backdrop is pure Americana — red dirt infield, green outfield wall, Oklahoma sky overhead.
BSB16.potentialContendersIntro: USA is the confirmed participant with a deep roster pool; Japan and four other nations will compete for medals through continental and World Cup qualification.

BSB17.blurb:
The afternoon session brings another round of preliminary games midway through the week. Teams settle into tournament routines as medal-round implications sharpen.

USA could appear in this window as they navigate the round-robin. Japan's defending champions will also cycle through the schedule, setting up potential rematches in the knockout stage.

Devon Park's 13,000 seats offer sightlines tailored for softball — short porches, close foul territory, no bad angles. It's a softball venue hosting an Olympic tournament, not an Olympic venue trying to host softball.
BSB17.potentialContendersIntro: The USA and Japan are projected as top contenders, with NCAA standouts like Mia Williams and NiJaree Canady potentially representing the host nation.

BSB18.blurb:
Prime time in OKC delivers another night session as the preliminary round passes its midpoint. The evening crowd brings energy, and teams feed off the atmosphere.

Rosters can carry 15 players aged 16 or older — coaches rotate lineups to manage arms and maximize matchups. Depth becomes an asset in a compressed five-day round-robin.

Devon Park sits in softball's spiritual home. The USA national team trains here, the Women's College World Series crowns champions here, and now Olympic softball returns here. The venue feels purpose-built for this moment.
BSB18.potentialContendersIntro: USA's roster pool includes emerging NCAA stars like Karlyn Pickens and NiJaree Canady; Japan brings the pedigree of Tokyo gold.

Qualification for LA28 includes a World Cup pathway and continental tournaments across Asia/Oceania, Europe/Africa, and the Americas. The six-team field will represent global softball's elite.

OKC Softball Park offers a fan experience rooted in the sport's culture — barbecue stands, open concourses, close proximity to the action. It's an Olympic event in a venue that lives and breathes softball year-round.

Afternoon round-robin games bring the preliminary round into its final stretch. Teams begin locking in playoff positions, and every pitch carries consequence.

The USA women have historically owned Olympic softball — four golds before Japan's Tokyo upset. A home crowd in Oklahoma adds pressure and fuel.

OKC Softball Park is America's premier softball-specific ballpark, hosting the Women's College World Series annually. The venue's history and softball pedigree create an atmosphere distinct from multipurpose Olympic sites.

USA seeks revenge for Tokyo's gold medal loss to Japan, but they'll need to navigate the round-robin first. Continental qualifiers from Europe/Africa, Asia/Oceania, and the Americas will fill out the six-team field.

Devon Park sits at USA Softball's headquarters complex. The organization's training center borders the venue, and the national team practices here regularly. For Team USA, this is a genuine home game.

Softball returns to the Olympics after skipping Paris 2024, giving LA28 a homecoming feel. The sport's eight-year absence makes this preliminary round a reintroduction for casual fans.

OKC Softball Park's intimacy suits the six-team format — you're never far from the action. The venue's softball-first design means optimal sightlines for every pitch, every swing, every diving catch.

OKC Softball Park has hosted top international competitions before, and the venue knows how to stage meaningful softball. Expect a crowd that understands the nuances — pitch selection, defensive shifts, situational hitting.

Softball returns to the Olympics after an eight-year gap, and OKC is the largest softball-specific stadium in America. The venue doubles as the Women's College World Series home.

Despite the Oklahoma location, softball teams may participate in LA ceremonies — a quirk of hosting events outside the main hub. The sport gets an Olympic platform, even if the geography is unconventional.

OKC Softball Park is the venue for the entire LA28 tournament — prelims, semis, and medal games all happen here. The sport gets a consistent home rather than bouncing between sites.

The venue's intimacy and softball pedigree create an authentic atmosphere. This is where the Women's College World Series crowns champions annually; now it hosts Olympic softball's return.

The bronze medal game is set for July 28, with the gold medal final on July 29. These last prelims determine who faces whom with hardware on the line.

Devon Park's 13,000-seat capacity and softball-specific design create an intimate, high-energy environment. The venue is USA Softball's headquarters home — a true homefield advantage for Team USA heading into the playoffs.

The preliminary round closes with prime-time games in OKC. Final standings are set, and the path to medals becomes clear.

This round-robin format runs from July 23 to 27, with three matches daily. It's a compact, intense schedule that tests depth and stamina before the knockout stage begins.

OKC Softball Park under the lights brings the preliminary round full circle. The venue has hosted the sport's biggest collegiate moments; now it delivers Olympic softball's return to the program after an eight-year absence.

BSB28.blurb:
The bronze medal game is softball's consolation prize, but there's no such thing as a throwaway match when Olympic hardware is on the line. One team will walk away with a medal; the other goes home empty-handed.

This session features the two teams that fell short in the semifinals, likely a mix of traditional powerhouses and emerging programs pushing for their first Olympic podium. The round-robin format means these squads have already faced each other once in pool play, adding familiarity and potential grudge-match energy.

OKC Softball Park is the sport's spiritual home in the United States, hosting the Women's College World Series every year and serving as USA Softball's headquarters. The 13,000-seat stadium brings an intimate, rowdy college atmosphere to the Olympic stage.

BSB29.blurb:
Softball's Olympic return hits its climax on home soil. Team USA enters as the most decorated program in the sport's history, but they're also carrying the weight of Tokyo, where Japan stunned them 2-0 in the gold medal game.

If the Americans make it through, this could be redemption on the grandest stage — the sport's spiritual headquarters, a Saturday morning crowd hungry for gold. The round-robin prelims narrow six teams to two finalists, so expect the world's best pitchers and power hitters going head-to-head with nothing held back.

OKC Softball Park seats 13,000, intimate enough that you'll hear every call from the dugout and feel every momentum swing. The venue hosts the Women's College World Series annually, so the crowd knows how to bring intensity.

CER01A.blurb:
The Games begin at SoFi Stadium in Inglewood, LA's gleaming modern landmark that hosted Super Bowl LVI and continues to redefine what a sports venue can be. Expect an opening that weaves Southern California's entertainment legacy with the pageantry of the Olympic movement — thousands of athletes parading in, a cauldron lighting, and performances calibrated for a global audience and a stadium built for spectacle.

This is the ceremonial handoff from Paris 2024 to LA28, complete with the parade of nations and the moment Team USA walks in as hosts. The show will likely lean into Hollywood, music, and the city's multicultural fabric, though creative details remain under wraps.

SoFi's canopy roof and wraparound Oculus screen turn the interior into an immersive theater. You'll feel the roar of 70,000 as the torch enters and the flame rises.

CER02.blurb:
The curtain falls at the LA Memorial Coliseum, the 1932 and 1984 Olympic host now closing the circle for a third LA Games. Seventeen days of competition culminate here with medal presentations, flag lowerings, and the formal handoff to the 2032 host city — a bittersweet exhale after the intensity of the fortnight.

The Coliseum's open-air bowl and torch perched above the peristyle give the ceremony a classical gravity. Expect athlete celebrations, musical performances, and the extinguishing of the flame as the Olympic flag passes to the next organizers.

You'll watch from historic stone seats under the Southern California sky, surrounded by a crowd that's witnessed every twist of these Games. The atmosphere tilts festive and reflective in equal measure.

CKT01.blurb:
Cricket returns to the Olympics after 128 years, and this is where it begins. The opening women's group stage session kicks off the T20 tournament at a purpose-built stadium in Pomona — compact, loud, intimate.

Six nations will compete in a round-robin format, with each team fielding 15 players in a fast-paced, explosive format designed for new audiences. The morning double-header sets the tone for the entire tournament.

Fairgrounds Cricket Stadium sits 50 kilometers east of downtown LA at the Pomona Fairplex. Temporary seating, close sightlines, and a South Asian diaspora crowd create an atmosphere more festival than traditional Olympic venue.
CKT01.potentialContendersIntro: No teams have officially qualified yet, but the ICC is developing a pathway to ensure top-performing nations compete. Australia Women are the reigning T20 World Cup champions from 2023 and expected to be among the favorites if they qualify.

potentialContenders.note: Reigning T20 World Cup champions (2023); dominant force in women's cricket with multiple World Cup titles

The evening session of opening day brings T20 cricket under the lights at Pomona. Two more group stage matches unfold in a format where momentum shifts in minutes and every over carries weight.

The double-header format means you'll see two complete matches in one sitting — roughly three hours of boundary-slamming, wicket-taking chaos. Teams are playing for group positioning that determines semifinal matchups.

Night cricket in Southern California creates a different energy than the morning session. The temporary stadium's lighting rig and close quarters amplify every six hit into the crowd.

Day two of the group stage continues the round-robin format. Each of the six teams will play five group matches total, making every result critical for advancing to the medal rounds.

T20 cricket is cricket's sprint format — 20 overs per side, roughly 90 minutes per innings. Batters swing early, bowlers attack, and matches turn on individual moments of brilliance or error.

The morning heat at the Pomona Fairplex makes for faster outfield conditions. Boundaries come easier, but so do run-outs when players push for quick singles on the synthetic turf.

CKT14.blurb:
The first Olympic cricket gold medal in 128 years. Two nations, 20 overs per side, winner takes the most historically significant trophy in the sport's return to the Games.

Women's cricket is experiencing explosive global growth, and this match could be transformative for the sport. The stakes transcend the Olympics — this is about legitimacy, funding, visibility, and the future of women's cricket worldwide.

Fairgrounds Stadium on the morning of July 20 will be the center of the cricket world. If India reaches this match, expect a crowd unlike anything else at LA28 — a billion people watching from home, tens of thousands at Pomona, all holding their breath.
CKT14.potentialContendersIntro: No teams have officially qualified yet, but Australia Women are the reigning T20 World Cup champions from 2024 and projected favorites if they qualify. India is expected to compete and would carry enormous cultural and political pressure to win gold. England, South Africa, and New Zealand are also likely contenders for the final.

The bronze medal match is cricket's consolation prize — bitter for the losers, meaningful for the winners. This is Olympic history: the first cricket medals awarded since 1900. Walking away with bronze beats walking away with nothing.

These are the semifinal losers, 24 hours removed from heartbreak, now grinding out one last match. Some teams will be devastated and flat. Others will rally for a podium finish and a place in the record books.

Morning start at Pomona means a quieter, more reflective atmosphere than the evening gold medal chaos to come. It's still Olympic cricket, still a medal on the line, still history being made after a 128-year gap.

CKT28.blurb:
The first Olympic cricket gold medal in 128 years. If India makes this final, a billion people will be watching. If Australia plays, they'll treat it like a World Cup final. If it's England, they'll call it their greatest T20 moment. This is the match cricket has been waiting for since 1900.

T20 finals are chaos — pressure bowling, boundary-or-bust hitting, captains burning timeouts and reviews. Twenty overs per side, three and a half hours, and then someone drapes a flag around their shoulders and stands on an Olympic podium with a gold medal.

Fairgrounds Stadium will be rocking. Morning start keeps it cool, but the atmosphere will be molten. LA's massive South Asian diaspora could turn Pomona into the loudest cricket ground on earth if India's playing. Whoever wins, it's history.

CLB01.blurb:
Twenty elite boulderers attack a fresh set of technical problems in the morning sun, with only six advancing to the final. Bouldering rewards creative problem-solving under pressure — climbers get four minutes per problem with unlimited attempts.

This is standalone bouldering, separated from lead for the first time since Tokyo. Paris combined champion Toby Roberts retired from bouldering in late 2025, opening the field. Qualification begins in 2027 with continental events and world championships allocating spots.

The Long Beach Climbing Theater is purpose-built for sightlines — the wall faces the crowd, no craning necks. Ocean breeze, outdoor atmosphere, intimate enough to hear climbers strategizing with coaches between burns.

CLB02.blurb:
Fourteen men and fourteen women each get one chance to fly up the 15-meter standardized wall. Fastest times advance to elimination rounds later in the week — a clean burn under 5.5 seconds typically makes the cut.

This is raw explosive power on display. Athletes launch from starting blocks in a crouch, hitting holds they've drilled thousands of times. One slip, one fraction-second hesitation, and you're watching from the stands.

Afternoon session under the California sun. The crowd is close enough to feel the tension in the qualification zone. Speed is visceral — you'll see world records threatened in real time.

CLB03.blurb:
The women's boulder semifinal narrows the field to six finalists. Fresh problems, new sequences, four minutes to solve puzzles that blend power, balance, and spatial reasoning.

Janja Garnbret won everything in Paris and remains the sport's gravitational center — if she competes, she's the favorite. But standalone bouldering draws specialists who've been sharpening for this split format since Tokyo. Qualification begins in 2027 at world championships and continental events.

Morning start under open sky at the Long Beach venue. The theater setup puts you eye-level with the crux moves, close enough to see chalk clouds and hear the crowd react to every dynamic lunge.

CLB04.blurb:
Lead climbing is endurance chess on a vertical wall. Athletes get six minutes and one attempt to climb as high as possible on a route they've never seen before — no practice burns, no second chances.

Adam Ondra retired from bouldering but continues chasing lead medals; at 35 he'd be the elder statesman if he qualifies. The semifinal will pare the field to six finalists for Thursday's medal round. Routes at this level are graded 8c to 9a — holds the size of credit card chips at 12 meters up.

Evening session with the wall lit against the twilight sky. Long Beach Climbing Theater's sightlines let you track every hand placement, every shake-out rest. When someone flashes past the crux, the roar echoes off the convention center lot.
CLB04.potentialContendersIntro: No LA28 qualifiers are confirmed yet, but recent results and current form point to probable semifinalists.

CLB05.blurb:
Both men's and women's speed champions will be crowned in a single 90-minute session. Quarterfinals, semifinals, finals — head-to-head elimination until two climbers race for gold.

Sam Watson holds the world record and won Paris gold; he's the hometown favorite. But speed is unforgiving — one slip in a five-second sprint and the medal goes to your opponent. Desiree Vos and Aleksandra Miroslaw have traded world records for years; their potential final would be a sprint for the ages.

Mid-morning energy, crowd packed in tight, athletes launching from starting blocks like sprinters. The standardized wall means no route-reading — just muscle memory and explosive power. Fifteen meters, five seconds, one shot.

CLB06.blurb:
The women's lead semifinal is six minutes of silent calculation repeated a dozen times. Climbers face a route they've only previewed from the ground — every hold choice, every rest position, improvised in real time.

Janja Garnbret won Paris combined and hasn't shown signs of slowing down. If she qualifies, she's the overwhelming favorite. But lead rewards experience and endurance; veterans who've memorized decades of sequences can outread younger power climbers. Qualification begins in 2027 at world championships and continental events.

Evening session with the wall lit against the dusk. Long Beach Climbing Theater puts you close enough to see grip adjustments and hear breathing. When someone locks off at the crux 12 meters up, the crowd holds its breath with them.

CLB07.blurb:
Six finalists, four brand-new boulder problems, two hours to determine the Olympic champion. Each problem gets four minutes and unlimited attempts — solve all four and you're probably holding gold.

Toby Roberts won Paris combined gold then retired from bouldering in 2025, leaving the field wide open. Expect Japanese and European specialists who've trained for standalone bouldering since the format split. Problems at this level blend crimps smaller than a dime with dynamic moves that require full-body commitment.

Morning final with ocean breeze and the crowd close enough to feel every attempt. Outdoor venue, chalk hanging in the air, athletes pacing between burns. When someone sticks the final hold on problem four, the theater erupts.

Four problems, six finalists, one morning to crown the women's boulder champion. This is problem-solving under pressure — athletes get four minutes per problem with unlimited tries to find the sequence.

Janja Garnbret has won nearly everything she's touched at Olympic level and remains active in competition. If she's on the starting list, she's the favorite. But bouldering punishes even the greats — one missed foot chip, one blown dyno, and the podium reshuffles.

Open-air venue with the Pacific Ocean as backdrop. The wall faces the crowd for unobstructed views of every dynamic move and delicate balance sequence. When someone tops the final problem, the morning session becomes a celebration.

Six finalists, one route, six minutes each to climb higher than the competition. Lead finals are chess matches played vertically — every rest position, every clip decision, calculated against the countdown clock.

Adam Ondra is aiming for this event after retiring from bouldering. At 35 he'd bring decades of route-reading experience against younger power climbers. The route will be graded somewhere between 8c and 9a — finger-width holds at 12 meters, no second chances.

Afternoon session with the wall fully lit. Long Beach Climbing Theater's outdoor setup means you're close enough to hear the rope click through quickdraws. When the leader reaches the crux and pauses to shake out, fifteen hundred people stop breathing.

CRD01.blurb:
The race of truth comes to LA in a single scorching session — men and women alone against the clock on a course looping out from the Coliseum. No peloton, no shelter, just pure power and aerodynamics over rolling Southern California roads.

Remco Evenepoel defended his world title in 2025 and took Paris gold; Filippo Ganna has been silver twice running and wants the top step. On the women's side, Chloe Dygert claimed the 2025 worlds after Paris bronze — her raw watts and home crowd could make the difference.

Expect packed grandstands near the start/finish ramp at the Coliseum, where you'll see riders launch and return at full threshold. The gaps between finishers tell the story in seconds, the margins ruthlessly thin.
CRD01.potentialContendersIntro: The world's elite time trialists are projected to compete, with recent world champions and Paris medalists likely headlining the start list.

CRD02.blurb:
The women's road race starts on the Venice Beach Boardwalk and unfolds over 150km of Southern California terrain. It's a mass-start endurance event where positioning, tactics, and timing matter as much as raw power — Paris 2024 proved that when Kristen Faulkner rode to a shock gold.

Ninety riders will chase Olympic glory through LA's diverse landscape. The course details remain under wraps, but expect climbs, descents, and technical sections that favor the versatile over the pure sprinters.

Venice Beach offers a quintessential LA atmosphere — palm trees, ocean breeze, street performers. The boardwalk start puts you right among the riders before they disappear into the city's hills and canyons.
CRD02.potentialContendersIntro: Kristen Faulkner won Paris 2024 on the roads of France. If she competes in LA, she'll race with home crowd energy and the weight of defending champion.

CRD03.blurb:
A 250km endurance test starting at Venice Beach and winding through LA. The men's road race is cycling's most unpredictable one-day classic — no team radios in the finale, no second chances, just strategy and survival.

Remco Evenepoel took Paris gold, but Tadej Pogacar won the Tour, Giro, and World Championships in 2024. If both line up in LA, it could be the most stacked Olympic road race field ever assembled.

The Venice boardwalk start gives spectators a relaxed vibe before the racing gets serious. You'll see the peloton roll out in the morning sun, then wait hours for the dramatic finish somewhere in the LA basin.

CSL01.blurb:
The first whitewater action of LA28 brings both men's and women's kayakers to Oklahoma City's man-made course for two timed runs through hanging gates. Paddlers attack the rapids solo, threading between poles without touching them — each contact adds penalty seconds that can sink a medal hope.

This is qualifying day, where the field gets cut and pressure builds. Athletes balance aggression with precision across two runs; only their best time counts.

The OKC Whitewater Center pumps 11,000 gallons per second through engineered drops and eddies. You'll hear the roar of the course before you see it — and watch paddlers read currents in real time from riverside viewing.

CSL02.blurb:
The women's kayak final crowns LA28's first canoe slalom champion. Twelve semifinalists get one high-stakes run; the fastest clean descent wins gold.

Jessica Fox is projected to defend her Paris title if she qualifies — she's won everything there is to win in this boat. But one gate touch, one missed line through a rapid, and the podium reshuffles completely.

Watch from the grandstands as paddlers drop into the course one at a time. The finish stretch passes directly below spectators — you'll feel the spray when they punch through the final rapid.

The OKC course runs through downtown's Boathouse District. Fans pack the banks and bridges, turning whitewater into a street festival with medal stakes.

Canoes return to the course — narrower boats, single-blade paddles, a completely different rhythm through the gates. Athletes kneel in the hull and use pure upper-body power to carve turns.

Both men and women run heats across the morning, two attempts each to post a qualifying time. The canoe is less forgiving than the kayak — you'll see more dramatic saves and occasionally spectacular swims.

This is the most technical day of qualifying, where course knowledge separates contenders from also-rans. The crowd sits close enough to hear paddlers grunt through the upstream gates.

CSL06.blurb:
The men's canoe final closes the traditional slalom program before kayak cross takes over. Single-blade paddlers kneel in narrow hulls, fighting upstream gates that demand perfect boat angle and explosive power.

Nicolas Gestin won Paris for France; Adam Burgess took silver for Britain. Both are projected to return if they qualify, but the canoe podium has historically been unpredictable.

This is the quietest, most focused session of the Games — pure athlete-versus-course. The OKC grandstands fill with slalom purists who appreciate the technical mastery on display.

Kayak cross debuted in Paris and instantly became the sport's most chaotic event — four paddlers racing head-to-head down whitewater, jostling for position, sometimes colliding. Round 1 sorts the field; the repechage gives eliminated racers one more shot.

This is slalom with contact allowed. Athletes launch off a ramp, sprint through gates, and execute an Eskimo roll mid-race. Strategy matters — drafting, blocking, choosing lines that force rivals wide.

The crowd energy shifts entirely from traditional slalom. Expect roaring, replays on the big screen, and constant action across back-to-back heats.

The second day of kayak cross heats continues sorting the bracket. Time trials ranked athletes for yesterday's matchups; now winners advance and losers face elimination or repechage pressure.

Four-boat racing means tactics evolve heat by heat — who takes the inside line at the first gate, who saves energy for the final sprint. You'll see both calculated racing and pure mayhem.

Friday morning at the Whitewater Center builds toward tomorrow's medal races. The course runs faster in qualifying; finalists will navigate tired water and tired bodies.

CSL09.blurb:
Canoe slalom's closing session is pure adrenaline — quarterfinals, semifinals, and both kayak cross finals packed into one morning. The last races of the Olympic whitewater program.

Medals come down to four-boat sprints where anything can happen. Paris saw last-second passes and photo finishes; expect the same chaos multiplied by a bracket's worth of grudges and tactical adjustments.

The OKC Whitewater Center will be rocking for the final session. Bring sunscreen, bring your voice, and watch paddlers launch off the start ramp one last time before the course goes quiet until 2032.
CSL09.potentialContendersIntro: Joseph Clarke (GBR) and Noemie Fox (AUS) are projected favorites if they qualify, having won Paris 2024 kayak cross golds. The format's unpredictability means underdogs can reach the podium with one great heat.

CSP01.blurb:
Morning racing in Long Beach harbor launches the Olympic canoe sprint programme with three events across multiple rounds. Heats and quarterfinals in men's kayak doubles, men's solo canoe, and women's solo canoe set the field for medal rounds later in the week.

The session spans distances from the explosive 200m women's C1 sprint to the endurance test of men's 1000m solo canoe. Qualification pressure makes early rounds unpredictable—crews must balance aggression with energy management across multiple races.

Marine Stadium offers spectators shoreline sightlines along the entire 1500m course, historic venue intimacy, and the chance to watch paddlers power stroke by stroke toward the finish pontoon.
CSP01.potentialContendersIntro: Expected contenders are based on Paris 2024 results and world rankings, with LA28 rosters still unconfirmed.

CSP02.blurb:
Four Olympic titles will be decided in a compact mid-morning finals session—men's kayak doubles, men's solo canoe, and their women's counterparts racing for gold. The men's C1 1000m final is canoe sprint's signature endurance test, while the K2 500m pairs explosive starts with tactical drafting.

Balint Kopasz of Hungary could defend his Paris gold in the K1 1000m if he qualifies for LA28. New Zealand's women often dominate kayak events; Brazil and China bring depth in men's canoe disciplines based on recent world rankings.

The Marine Stadium grandstand fills early for medal sessions. Watch finalists dig deep in the final 250 meters as the Long Beach crowd roars and photo-finish technology decides tight races at the pontoon.
CSP02.potentialContendersIntro: Likely finalists are projected from Paris 2024 and World Championship results, pending official LA28 qualification.

CSP03.blurb:
The busiest session of the regatta packs six events into a four-and-a-half-hour window—heats and quarterfinals in four-person kayaks, two-person canoes, and the marquee solo races. Lisa Carrington could appear in women's K1 500m if she competes at LA28, racing the distance where she's won multiple Olympic golds.

The K4 500m events showcase synchronized power as four paddlers move in perfect rhythm. Men's K1 1000m heats begin the tournament's centerpiece event, while women's C2 debuts the newest Olympic canoe discipline with qualification races.

Long Beach's tight course lets spectators track boats from start to finish. The bridge spanning the channel adds a visual landmark; crew boats and harbor traffic pause during racing windows.
CSP03.potentialContendersIntro: Projected entries draw from recent world rankings and Paris 2024 performances, with LA28 qualification still ahead.

CSP04.blurb:
Three Olympic champions will be crowned in rapid succession as women's K1 500m, women's C2 500m, and men's K1 1000m reach their medal conclusions. The women's K1 could feature Lisa Carrington chasing a sixth career Olympic gold if she qualifies, while the men's K1 1000m final delivers canoe sprint's most prestigious title.

Balint Kopasz enters as the reigning men's K1 champion from Paris, though his LA28 participation awaits confirmation. Women's C2 500m remains the newest Olympic event—only its second Games since debuting in Tokyo—with China and Canada expected to field strong crews.

Finals morning at Marine Stadium means tight racing and podium ceremonies shoreside. Medal races often come down to hundredths of a second, settled by photo technology as athletes collapse over their boats at the line.
CSP04.potentialContendersIntro: Medal favorites are projected from Paris 2024 and current form, with official LA28 rosters pending qualification.

CSP05.blurb:
The regatta closes with four finals spanning canoe sprint's full spectrum—the lightning 200m women's solo sprint, men's canoe doubles, and the thundering eight-person K4 races. Women's C1 200m could see Nevin Harrison race for gold on home water after her Paris silver medal.

K4 500m finals are pure spectacle: four paddlers in perfect sync, boats knifing through flat water at top speed, national pride on the line. Germany and New Zealand often field dominant K4 crews based on World Championship results, though LA28 lineups remain unconfirmed.

Closing session atmosphere at Marine Stadium combines medal stakes with regatta-finale energy. The compact harbor venue keeps spectators close to the racing pontoons as Olympic canoe sprint crowns its final champions of the Games.
CSP05.potentialContendersIntro: Projected finalists draw from Paris 2024 and recent world rankings, pending LA28 qualification outcomes.

CTR01.blurb:
The Carson Velodrome roars to life with the first track cycling session of the Games — men's and women's team sprint qualifying. Three riders launch from a standing start, hitting 85 km/h on the steep wooden banking as they chase Olympic glory.

Team sprint is pure speed: a timed pursuit with no head-to-head drama, just riders redlining for under 50 seconds. The Netherlands men and New Zealand women arrive as defending Paris champions, but qualifying determines which nations advance to the medal rounds later in the day.

The brand-new velodrome — North America's only permanent international-standard indoor track — seats spectators right above the action. You'll hear the snap of carbon wheels, smell the wood, and feel the building shake when a team goes full gas.
CTR01.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024 medalists are projected to return, though LA28 rosters remain unconfirmed. Watch for defending champions and rising sprint powers.

CTR02.blurb:
Three hours, three finals. The team sprint medals are decided in head-to-head knockout rounds — riders battling side-by-side on opposite straights of the velodrome. Then the men's team pursuit qualifying begins: four-rider pacelines hunting perfect aerodynamic sync across 16 laps.

The team sprint final is a 45-second explosion; the pursuit is a four-minute chess match of positioning and power. If the Netherlands men or New Zealand women qualify from the morning session, they'll race for gold here. The British squads, perennial pursuiting powers, could challenge for medals in both events.

Carson's steep banking amplifies every acceleration. The crowd noise bounces off the wood, and the finish-line photo boards light up before riders have crossed.
CTR02.potentialContendersIntro: Paris champions are expected to return for team sprint; pursuit fields remain open as LA28 qualification isn't finalized. Recent world results suggest contenders.

CTR03.blurb:
The velodrome hosts a marathon morning session stitching together four events. Women's team pursuit qualifying sees four-rider trains chasing times to reach the medal rounds; men's team pursuit hits its first knockout stage; women's keirin opens with heats and repechages.

Keirin is track cycling's controlled chaos: a motorbike paces six riders for 5.5 laps, then peels off and leaves them to sprint. Ellesse Andrews of New Zealand won Paris gold; Emma Finucane took Paris sprint bronze and could be a keirin threat if she qualifies for LA28. The men's sprint qualifying wraps the session — 200-meter flying laps to seed the knockout bracket.

This is the longest track session of the Games, nearly three and a half hours. The Carson velodrome gets loud when keirin riders jockey for position behind the derny motorbike.
CTR03.potentialContendersIntro: Keirin contenders are drawn from Paris results and recent World Cup form, though LA28 participation isn't confirmed. Sprint and pursuit fields remain speculative.

CTR04.blurb:
The men's team pursuit final closes the pursuit competition — four riders hunting the ultimate paceline over 4,000 meters. Great Britain won Paris gold and defended their world title in 2025, making them the likely favorite if they qualify.

Before the pursuit final, the men's sprint knockout rounds begin: 1/32 and 1/16 finals, three-lap match sprints where riders shadow and feint before exploding in the final 200 meters. Harrie Lavreysen, the world number one and Paris triple gold medalist, could appear if he advances from morning qualifying. The women's keirin quarterfinals follow — eight riders, two heats, everything on the line.

Carson's sightlines put you directly above the banking. When two sprinters hit 70 km/h in the final lap, you see the tactical game unfold in real time.
CTR04.potentialContendersIntro: Great Britain's pursuit dominance and Lavreysen's sprint supremacy are grounded in recent results; their LA28 participation is projected but not confirmed. Keirin quarterfinalists depend on morning outcomes.

CTR05.blurb:
Five hours, four finals, one massive session. The women's team pursuit medals are decided; the men's omnium runs all four disciplines (scratch race, tempo, elimination, points race) in a single afternoon; the women's keirin crowns a champion.

The omnium is track cycling's decathlon — endurance, tactics, and sprinting combined. Riders accumulate points across events, and the winner is whoever's sharpest across all formats. Meanwhile, the men's sprint knockout bracket continues (1/8 finals, quarterfinals), and the women's keirin builds from semis to final in under 30 minutes of explosive racing.

This is the longest, densest session on the track calendar. The Carson crowd will be exhausted and electrified in equal measure by the closing keirin final.
CTR05.potentialContendersIntro: Keirin finalists are based on Paris and recent form; omnium contenders remain speculative as LA28 rosters aren't set. Sprint knockouts depend on qualifying outcomes.

CTR06.blurb:
The men's sprint semifinals — two match sprints per rider, best-of-three if needed — decide who races for gold later in the day. Harrie Lavreysen, the reigning Paris champion and world number one, is projected to reach this stage if he qualifies for LA28.

Before the semis, the women's sprint competition begins: qualifying and 1/32 finals, with repechages for riders who miss direct advancement. It's a long morning of timed 200-meter laps and early knockout rounds, seeding the bracket that leads to medals on Sunday.

Carson Velodrome is North America's only permanent international-standard indoor track. The steep banking and tight corners reward tactical patience — riders who can hold the inside line and time their jump win sprints.
CTR06.potentialContendersIntro: Men's sprint semifinalists are projected from Paris results and current world rankings; women's sprint field is speculative as LA28 rosters remain unannounced.

CTR07.blurb:
The men's sprint final — three laps, best two of three rides, the most iconic duel in track cycling. Two riders shadow each other at walking pace, jockeying for position, then explode to 70 km/h in a final-lap sprint. Harrie Lavreysen won Paris gold and could return if he qualifies.

The women's madison final runs concurrently: pairs of riders racing 30 km with mid-race sprints every 10 laps, hand-slinging teammates into the action. It's relay-style chaos across 120 laps, and points stack fast. The session closes with women's sprint 1/16 finals and repechages, advancing the knockout bracket.

Carson's capacity crowd sits directly above the finish line. When two sprinters come off the final banking at full speed, the roar is deafening.
CTR07.potentialContendersIntro: Men's sprint finalists are projected from Paris and world rankings; madison pairs remain speculative as LA28 rosters aren't finalized. Expect defending champions if they qualify.

CTR08.blurb:
The men's madison final — 50 km, 200 laps, two-rider teams hand-slinging each other into sprints every 20 laps. Points accumulate in bursts, and the lead can flip five times in the final 10 minutes. It's endurance racing meets strategic chess, and the crowd tracking is half the fun.

Before the madison, the women's sprint knockout rounds continue (1/8 finals, quarterfinals), and the men's keirin opens with first-round heats and repechages. Keirin is the velodrome's wildcard event: six riders paced by a motorbike, then three laps of absolute chaos. Harrie Lavreysen won Paris gold in keirin and sprint — if he qualifies, he could chase double gold again.

Carson's wooden track amplifies the sound of 12 madison riders sprinting in a pack. When teams collide or crash, you feel it in the stands.
CTR08.potentialContendersIntro: Madison pairs and keirin contenders are drawn from Paris results and current form; LA28 rosters aren't confirmed. Lavreysen is the projected keirin favorite if he competes.

CTR09.blurb:
The final track session of the Games is a medal bonanza: women's omnium all four rounds, women's sprint semifinals and final, men's keirin quarterfinals through final. Nearly four hours of nonstop racing closes the velodrome.

The omnium crowns the women's most versatile rider across scratch, tempo, elimination, and points races. The women's sprint final is best-of-three match sprints — Ellesse Andrews and Emma Finucane could meet if both qualify and advance. The men's keirin final is three laps of pure chaos, six riders sprinting for gold after a motorbike paces them to 50 km/h.

Carson Velodrome's steep banking and tight sightlines make this the loudest session of the Games. When the final keirin riders hit the bell lap, the building shakes.
CTR09.potentialContendersIntro: Sprint and keirin finalists are projected from Paris results and recent form; omnium contenders remain speculative as LA28 rosters aren't finalized.

DIV01.blurb:
The women's springboard preliminary kicks off LA28 diving under California sun at the Rose Bowl Aquatics Center. This session winnows a global field down to the 18 semifinalists who'll advance — an accessible entry point to watch the sport's rising stars.

Expect divers from powerhouse nations like China, Australia, and Great Britain to set the pace early. The springboard rewards rhythm and precision, with athletes executing five dives of varying difficulty.

The outdoor venue puts you close to the action, with the San Gabriel Mountains framing every splash. Morning light on the water and the crack of the board create an atmosphere no indoor pool can match.
DIV01.potentialContendersIntro: Rosters won't be finalized until 2027 qualification events, but recent world championship and Paris 2024 results suggest likely contenders.

Eighteen divers become twelve. The semifinal cuts the field to the evening final's medal contenders, raising the stakes with every splash.

China's springboard depth could put multiple divers through, while Australia and Great Britain likely field strong challengers based on recent Paris results. Each athlete performs five dives — one mistake can end an Olympic dream.

The midday session at Pasadena's purpose-built aquatics venue offers prime viewing of the 3-meter board. The outdoor setting amplifies the drama as divers fight for a spot in the final twelve.

Twelve divers. Five rounds. One gold medal decided by millimeters of splash. The women's springboard final is diving's most accessible spectacle — the 3-meter height puts the crowd right on top of the action.

China has dominated this event historically, but the field has deepened in recent cycles. Expect the final round to come down to execution under pressure, with the slightest ripple costing podium position.

The late-afternoon session at the Rose Bowl Aquatics Center bathes the platform in golden light. The outdoor amphitheater setting means you'll feel every gasp and cheer as medals are decided.

The men's springboard preliminary opens with a deep international field vying for eighteen semifinal spots. This is where unknowns announce themselves and favorites confirm their form.

China's Xie Siyi won Paris gold and remains nearly unbeatable on the springboard — if he competes, he'll set the standard. But springboard diving rewards consistency over power, creating openings for precise technicians from any nation.

The morning session offers an intimate view of the sport's mechanics. Outdoor acoustics at the Rose Bowl Aquatics Center mean you'll hear every board crack and water entry from your seat.

Xie Siyi's Paris victory and consistent dominance make him the projected favorite if he qualifies for LA28. But the springboard punishes the slightest hesitation — one missed entry and the podium reshuffles.

DIV07.blurb:
The women's platform preliminary begins the most dominant streak in Olympic diving. China has won this event at every Games since 2008 — expect that dynasty to shape the entire session.

Quan Hongchan's near-perfect Paris gold at age 17 established her as a generational talent. If she and training partner Chen Yuxi both qualify, their rivalry alone will define the event. The 10-meter height separates the fearless from the cautious.

Morning sun illuminates the 33-foot platform against the San Gabriel Mountains. The outdoor setting amplifies the height — you'll feel the vertigo from the stands as divers launch into California sky.

Quan Hongchan and Chen Yuxi could turn this into a preview of their projected final battle, assuming both qualify through 2027 events. Their Paris rivalry captivated the sport. But platform diving punishes overthinking — the 10-meter height exposes every hesitation.

Australia's Cassiel Rousseau is one of the few who has consistently challenged Chinese dominance on platform. If he qualifies, expect him to push the difficulty envelope. The prelim's wide-open field means breakout performances from unexpected nations.

The outdoor Rose Bowl setting makes the platform's height visceral. The San Gabriel Mountains frame every dive — you'll watch athletes disappear against the skyline before gravity takes over.

The women's 10-meter platform final is the most anticipated session in diving. China has won gold here at every Olympics since 2008 — the question is which Chinese diver claims it.

Quan Hongchan's Paris performance at 17 was nearly flawless. Chen Yuxi, her training partner, won Tokyo gold. If both qualify and compete, their rivalry becomes the session's centerpiece — two divers who know each other's every move, battling from 33 feet up.

The evening session captures diving under California sky, with the Rose Bowl's outdoor amphitheater creating an electric atmosphere. You'll watch history extend or finally break as medals are decided.

Twelve men survive to the semifinal. Twelve dives stand between them and tomorrow's gold-medal session. The 10-meter platform rewards courage — hesitation from 33 feet costs medals.

Cassiel Rousseau finished fourth in Paris and projects as Australia's best hope to challenge Chinese depth if he qualifies. The semifinal's intensity ratchets up as divers attempt their riskiest maneuvers to secure finalist spots.

The men's 10-meter platform final closes individual diving at LA28. From 33 feet up, one splash determines Olympic gold — the most dramatic moment in the sport.

China's platform dominance extends to the men, though the field has proven more open than the women's event. Cassiel Rousseau's Paris fourth-place finish positions him as a projected challenger if he qualifies. Expect the final round to come down to nerve under pressure.

The afternoon session captures diving in perfect Pasadena light. The Rose Bowl's outdoor venue amplifies the stakes — every person in the stands will hold their breath as the final divers climb the ladder.

DIV13.blurb:
Two divers. Perfect synchronization. The women's synchronized springboard demands telepathic precision — partners must mirror each other's every rotation and entry from separate boards.

China won Paris gold with Chang Yani and Chen Yuxi, while Australia's Keeney and Smith took silver. Synchro teams will be determined at 2028 World Cup stops, making rosters pure projection. The U.S. receives a host-nation spot but faces steep competition.

The midday session puts you close enough to see partners' subtle timing cues. The outdoor Rose Bowl setting creates an intimate theater for synchronized artistry.

DIV14.blurb:
Synchronized diving from the 3-meter board is where athleticism meets choreography. Two athletes must execute identical dives simultaneously — any mismatch in timing or form costs points.

China's Long Daoyi and Wang Zongyuan won Paris gold and project as favorites if the partnership continues through qualification. Synchro rosters won't be finalized until 2028 World Cup events. The U.S. gets a host-nation berth but faces elite competition.

The afternoon session at the Rose Bowl Aquatics Center offers clear sightlines of both boards. The outdoor venue's intimacy means you'll catch every synchronized detail as medals are decided.

DIV15.blurb:
Two divers synchronized from 33 feet up — the platform's height makes every mismatch brutally visible. The women's synchro final demands partners who can read each other's timing mid-flight.

China's depth on platform could produce a formidable pairing if Quan Hongchan or Chen Yuxi compete in synchro alongside individual events. Synchro teams won't be determined until 2028 World Cup stops. The U.S. receives a host berth but faces China's projected dominance.

The afternoon session offers dramatic sightlines of synchronized flight against the San Gabriel Mountains. The outdoor Rose Bowl venue creates a natural amphitheater for watching two athletes move as one.

DIV16.blurb:
Two divers launch from 33 feet in perfect unison, their bodies tracing identical arcs against the San Gabriel Mountains. The synchronized 10m platform final is diving's ultimate test of trust and precision — partners must match each other's height, rotation, and entry down to the centimeter.

China won gold at Paris 2024 and has claimed nearly every synchronized platform medal this century, but the outdoor Rose Bowl setting could reshape the mental game. Wind, sunlight, and the electric atmosphere make outdoor synchro notoriously unpredictable.

You'll watch from tiered seating that wraps the pool, close enough to hear the divers' calls to each other before launch. Every splash sends up a wall of California sunshine, and the crowd reacts to each score in real time.
DIV16.potentialContendersIntro: China is projected to field the gold-medal favorite pairing, while the host United States receives an automatic quota spot and could present a dark-horse team.

EQU01.blurb:
Eventing begins with dressage, the elegant phase where horse and rider perform a set choreography in the arena. Teams and individuals compete in the same rounds—each ride counts toward both medals.

This morning session opens the three-day test. Riders showcase precision and partnership under the San Gabriel Mountains backdrop.

Santa Anita Park, a storied thoroughbred racing venue since 1934, transforms into Olympic equestrian headquarters. The historic grandstand offers sweeping views of the dressage arena below.

EQU02.blurb:
Day one's evening session continues the eventing dressage phase. Teams need three strong rides to advance—one weak score can sink medal hopes.

The afternoon light filters through the San Gabriels as late-starting nations take their turn. Each test is judged on accuracy, rhythm, and the invisible harmony between horse and rider.

Summer evenings at Santa Anita bring cooler air and golden-hour light. The tiered seating puts you close enough to hear hoofbeats on the turf.

EQU03.blurb:
Eventing dressage concludes Sunday morning with the final rides of the opening phase. Overnight scores reshape the leaderboard—a flawless test here can vault a rider into medal position.

Countries holding back their strongest combinations often deploy them now. Judges look for technical precision: a horse that bends cleanly through corners, halts square, maintains tempo.

The morning crowd at Santa Anita includes serious equestrian fans who arrive early to watch warm-up rings. You'll see the invisible work that precedes each test.

EQU04.blurb:
Cross-country is eventing's heart-pounding phase. Horse and rider gallop a miles-long course over solid obstacles—log piles, water complexes, earthen banks—at speed.

Santa Anita's infield transforms into a technical cross-country track. One refusal or a fall can end medal dreams; clean rounds within the time allowed are pure adrenaline.

Stand near a combination fence to feel the ground shake as horses thunder past. The crowd migrates between obstacles, chasing the action across the course.

EQU05.blurb:
Eventing ends with show jumping—tired horses and riders navigate a course of colored rails after two grueling days. One pole down costs four faults; medals hang on fractions of a second.

Team and individual finals run together in a single evening session. Nations chase the podium first, then the top 25 individuals jump again for their own medals.

Prime-time under Santa Anita's lights, the crowd roars at clean rounds and groans at fallen rails. It's the most accessible equestrian discipline—you don't need to know dressage to understand the stakes.

EQU06.blurb:
Dressage proper begins—not the eventing warm-up, but the sport's pure form. Horses perform the Grand Prix, a test of the highest-level movements: piaffe, passage, pirouettes.

Germany and Great Britain have dominated recent championships. Britain won team gold at Paris 2024 but fields new riders after Charlotte Dujardin's suspension. Jessica von Bredow-Werndl of Germany, Tokyo individual champion, is expected to compete.

Santa Anita's dressage arena sits in the infield, ringed by seating that puts you close to the action. You'll hear the rhythm of hoofbeats, the cues riders whisper.

EQU07.blurb:
The afternoon session continues Grand Prix qualifying. Scores determine which teams advance to Saturday's Special and which individuals reach Monday's Freestyle final.

Each ride is seven minutes of controlled power. Judges score on a scale to 10 for each movement—an 8 is excellent, a 9 is rare, a 10 is nearly mythical.

Late-day sessions at Santa Anita can be breezy. Dressage horses are sensitive; wind and shadows test their training. Watch for the subtle corrections riders make.

EQU08.blurb:
Day two of Grand Prix qualifying opens Friday morning. Overnight scores have set benchmarks—riders know what they need to advance.

Dressage at this level is the result of years of partnership. Horses are often 12 to 15 years old, their riders in their 30s or 40s. You're watching the peak of a decade-long journey.

Santa Anita's morning light is clear and direct. The mountain backdrop frames the white-fenced arena. It's quieter than evening sessions—purists come early.

Grand Prix qualifying concludes Friday afternoon. The final rides lock in team standings and individual rankings—no second chances.

Countries save their closers for this session. A single brilliant test can lift a team to the podium; a mistake drops them out of medals.

The late-afternoon crowd knows the stakes. Every extended trot and flying lead change draws murmurs from the knowledgeable fans who fill Santa Anita's boxes.

The Grand Prix Special decides team medals. Only the top eight teams from Thursday and Friday advance; their top three rides here count.

Britain won gold at Paris 2024 with a new generation after Charlotte Dujardin's suspension. Germany took silver, led by Jessica von Bredow-Werndl. Both nations are projected to contend again.

Saturday morning at Santa Anita Park, the iconic racing venue that's hosted champions since 1934. The team final draws serious crowds—national pride on the line.

Team dressage concludes in the afternoon session. The final rides from the top nations determine gold, silver, and bronze.

Each team's third rider carries the weight of their nation. One blown movement—a break in rhythm, a missed transition—can cost the podium.

Late-afternoon tension at Santa Anita as medals are decided. The grandstand fills with flags and chants. When the final scores post, you'll see tears—of joy or heartbreak.

Dressage Freestyle is the sport's artistic pinnacle. The top 18 individuals ride to music they've chosen, choreographing movements to match the rhythm and mood.

Jessica von Bredow-Werndl won this event at Tokyo, her horse TSF Dalera dancing to a custom score. Britain's Charlotte Fry, Paris team gold medalist, could be a contender if she qualifies.

Monday morning under Santa Anita's open sky, the final equestrian medals awarded. Each freestyle is a four-minute performance—part sport, part ballet. The crowd claps along to the music.

Show jumping shifts to center stage. Teams of three riders each jump a technical course—verticals, oxers, triple combinations—aiming for zero faults.

This qualifier determines which nations reach Wednesday's final. Steve Guerdat of Switzerland, 2012 Olympic champion, may still compete at age 52. Sweden, Belgium, and the Netherlands historically field strong jumping squads.

Santa Anita's jump course is built fresh—bright rails, tight turns. Sit near the in-gate to watch riders walk the course beforehand, memorizing distances.

Team show jumping medals are won and lost in two hours. The top ten teams from Tuesday return; each nation's three riders jump, faults accumulate, and the podium takes shape.

France won gold at Paris 2024. Sweden took silver. Both are expected to field strong teams again, though rosters won't finalize until spring 2028.

Wednesday morning's final is pure pressure. Rails fall in slow motion, the crowd gasps, and fortunes reverse with every round. Santa Anita's grandstand will be packed.

Individual show jumping qualifying spans a long morning as the top 75 horse-and-rider pairs worldwide chase 30 final spots. Two rounds, faults and time combined.

Steve Guerdat of Switzerland won Olympic gold in 2012 and still competes at the highest level—if he qualifies, he'd be one of the oldest show jumpers in Games history. This qualifier is a marathon: four hours of rails, refusals, and time faults.

Friday at Santa Anita with the individual final looming Monday. Early risers get the best seats; the crowd builds as medal contenders take the ring late in the session.

EQU16.blurb:
Show jumping's individual final is pure nerve — every rail counts, every refusal reshuffles the podium. Riders navigate a technical course of jumps at Santa Anita Park, where a single mistake costs four faults and Olympic dreams evaporate in seconds.

The format is unforgiving: clear rounds advance to a jump-off against the clock, turning precision into a speed chess match on horseback. Expect roaring crowds as riders thread the line between caution and risk, horses soaring over oxers and verticals in a sport where millimeters separate gold from heartbreak.

Santa Anita's storied grounds — home to thoroughbred racing since 1934 — become an Olympic arena with the San Gabriel Mountains rising beyond the fences. You'll feel every hoof-beat, hear the gasp when a rail teeters, watch partnerships forged over years prove themselves in two minutes of flight.

FBL01.blurb:
The Olympic men's tournament kicks off at Etihad Park, New York City FC's gleaming new home rising in the Bronx. This is U-23 soccer with edge — three overage wildcards per squad, group stage stakes that can eliminate a nation before the Opening Ceremony even happens.

Twelve teams, three groups of four, 18 matches scattered from coast to coast before the knockout rounds converge on California. Spain arrives as reigning gold medalists from Paris; France carried home silver and the sting of a final-day loss; Argentina's youth conveyor belt keeps cranking out talent that makes the senior squad look over its shoulder.

Etihad Park seats 25,000 in a purpose-built soccer cathedral. The sightlines are steep and tight, the atmosphere electric when a young phenom announces himself on the Olympic stage.
FBL01.potentialContendersIntro: Spain U-23 won gold at Paris 2024, France took silver, and Argentina's youth pipeline continues to produce world-class talent. Exact rosters and group assignments await the official draw.
