FBL02.blurb:
A second men's group stage session at Etihad Park — double-header day in the Bronx. The Olympic format compresses group play into a tight window, so venues host multiple sessions to churn through 18 preliminary matches before quarterfinals begin.

U-23 rosters mean faces you might not recognize yet — but check back in four years when they're headlining World Cup squads. Olympic soccer is where Neymar, Messi, and Mbappé first wore the five rings. The three overage slots let nations bring one or two veterans to anchor the kids.

New York's soccer-mad diaspora will pack the stands. Etihad Park is built for this — steep rake, no track separating fans from the pitch, noise that bounces off the roof and onto the field.

FBL03.blurb:
The men's Olympic tournament opens in Columbus — Crew Stadium, a Midwest soccer stronghold, hosting the first whistle of LA28's beautiful game. Twelve U-23 squads plus three overage veterans each, 18 group stage matches before the knockout rounds converge on California.

Spain arrives as Paris gold medalists, France as silver medalists nursing the sting of a home final loss, Argentina as the latest iteration of a youth system that never stops producing. The group stage is unforgiving — lose your opener and you're chasing points; draw twice and goal differential might send you home early.

Scotts Miracle-Gro Field is the Columbus Crew's home, a purpose-built soccer venue where the supporters' section knows the songs and the sightlines put you on top of the action. Olympic soccer in July, Midwest summer heat, and the roar when a goal goes in.

A second men's group stage session in Columbus — Scotts Miracle-Gro Field hosting back-to-back matches as the tournament churns through its preliminary rounds. The format is tight: 12 teams, three groups of four, 18 matches before the quarterfinals begin.

U-23 rosters mean young legs, hungry players, and the occasional overage veteran brought in to steady the ship. Spain's Paris squad played tiki-taka at hyperspeed; France had 80,000 in Paris and still couldn't close the deal; Argentina keeps sending youth teams that punch above their collective age.

The Crew's home is a soccer-specific stadium — steep stands, no running track, crowd noise funneled straight onto the pitch. When Columbus hosts a match that matters, the atmosphere rivals any venue in the country.

Geodis Park, home of Nashville SC, is one of the newest soccer-specific stadiums in North America. Intimate sightlines and a dedicated supporter section make this feel like a club match with Olympic stakes.

FBL09.blurb:
The women's tournament opens in Columbus before the Olympic flame reaches LA — early start to give players recovery time. Scotts Miracle-Gro Field, home of the Crew, hosts a group stage session with real stakes: 16 teams, four groups, top two advance.

FBL38.blurb:
The final women's group stage matches — elimination Monday. Sixteen teams started, eight advance to quarterfinals, and the margins are razor-thin. A draw might not be enough; goal differential could decide who flies to the knockout rounds and who flies home.

The USWNT defends Paris gold with a roster still to be finalized — but on home soil, expectations are sky-high. Spain's 2023 World Cup title and current depth make them the team everyone measures against. Great Britain, if they feature England's Euro champions, brings pedigree and tactical discipline. The group stage is where favorites can stumble before the world is watching.

Etihad Park seats 25,000 in a soccer-specific bowl. The stands wrap tight around the pitch, the sightlines are steep, and when a match matters the noise carries weight.

FBL39.blurb:
Final round of women's group stage at PayPal Park. Win-and-advance scenarios dominate: some teams clinch quarterfinal spots, others fight for survival, a few face elimination regardless.

Final group matches kick off simultaneously to prevent tactical gamesmanship. Teams can't sit back and wait for scores elsewhere; they must play their own match and hope the math works out.

The smallest Olympic soccer venue delivers maximum drama when elimination looms. PayPal Park's 18,000 will roar or groan as every shot and save reshapes the bracket.

FBL41.blurb:
Matchday two of the group stage — win and you control your fate, lose and the math gets tight. Teams that stumbled in their opener come out desperate; teams that won play to lock down qualification early.

Sixteen-team groups mean every result ripples. A draw can be a disaster or a lifeline depending on the other match in your group. The tournament's storylines start to clarify here — who's vulnerable, who's dangerous, who might not make it out.

Snapdragon Stadium delivers an intimate soccer atmosphere. The stands are close, the acoustics loud, and San Diego's soccer culture means the crowd knows when to roar.

The final group stage window — do-or-die for teams on the bubble, victory lap for those already through. Four groups finish simultaneously, so the permutations multiply: a goal in one match can knock out a team in another.

This is where Olympic soccer gets ruthless. Sixteen teams enter the groups, eight go home before the knockouts even start. Coaches hedge, players calculate, and late goals rewrite entire brackets.

Snapdragon Stadium's 35,000 seats make it the largest of the new venues outside LA. The energy peaks here when elimination is on the line.

FBL46.blurb:
Men's quarterfinal at Etihad Park — win or go home. The group stage winnowed twelve teams to eight; this round cuts it to four. No more feeling out opponents, no more points math. Ninety minutes, maybe extra time, maybe penalties, and one team advances to the semis while the other watches the rest of the tournament from home.

Spain won Paris gold with a U-23 squad that dismantled France in the final. France, now without home-crowd advantage, will be hungry to reclaim the summit. Argentina's youth pipeline means they could field a roster that looks nothing like Paris and still be dangerous. But quarterfinals are where pedigree meets the moment — and the team that handles pressure better usually wins.

Etihad Park is a 25,000-seat soccer cathedral in the Bronx. Steep stands, tight sightlines, crowd noise that reverberates through the bowl. When the stakes are a semifinal berth, the atmosphere is suffocating.

FBL47.blurb:
The Rose Bowl hosts a women's quarterfinal — the first knockout match at the most iconic soccer venue in American history. Eight teams remain, one will advance to the semifinals, one goes home.

Quarterfinals are where Olympic soccer gets ruthless. Ninety minutes, maybe extra time, maybe penalties. No margin for error. The Rose Bowl has seen World Cup drama; now it gets Olympic stakes.

The stadium seats nearly 90,000, and if the USWNT is playing here the atmosphere will be seismic. Even without the home team, a quarterfinal at the Rose Bowl is a bucket-list soccer experience.

Geodis Park will be electric for a women's quarterfinal. The crowd, the stakes, the summer heat — this is where Olympic legends are born.

The Rose Bowl at night for a men's Olympic semifinal — this is the game where stars are born. U-23 squads loaded with future World Cup heroes, ninety minutes from a gold medal match, playing under the lights at the stadium that hosted the 1994 World Cup final.

Olympic men's soccer is where Messi won his first major trophy, where Neymar silenced doubters, where global superstars proved themselves. The semifinals are the inflection point — reach the final and you're a legend, lose and you're forgotten.

The Rose Bowl holds over 90,000, and for a primetime semifinal it will be deafening. The west coast start time means this match kicks off while the rest of the country is still awake.

This session starts at 5:30 PM Pacific, which means the sun sets over the stadium during play. Snapdragon's west-facing bowl catches the golden hour beautifully — and then the lights come on for the finish.

FBL55.blurb:
The bronze medal match is the cruelest game in the Olympics — you were ninety minutes from gold two days ago, now you're fighting for third. Some teams treat it as consolation; others play with nothing left to lose.

But a medal is a medal, and for U-23 players this might be the only Olympic podium they ever reach. The match often opens up tactically, with both sides willing to gamble. It can produce spectacular soccer.

Snapdragon Stadium under the lights on a Thursday evening. The losing semifinalists have forty-eight hours to regroup, reframe, and leave San Diego with bronze.

FBL56.blurb:
Bronze is bittersweet — two days ago these teams were dreaming of gold, now they're playing to leave LA with a medal instead of regret. The women's bronze match has produced some of the tournament's most emotional moments.

Teams that lose semifinals often arrive here exhausted, heartbroken, unsure how to summon one more performance. But Olympic bronze is still history, still a podium, still a memory that lasts forever.

Snapdragon Stadium at midday, the final soccer match in San Diego before the tournament moves to the Rose Bowl for the finals. The crowd sends the Games off with one last roar.

The men's Olympic final at the Rose Bowl — the same stadium that hosted the 1994 World Cup final, the most-attended World Cup match ever played. Two U-23 teams, ninety minutes, one gold medal.

Olympic men's soccer launches careers. Messi finally won a major trophy here. Neymar carried Brazil to home gold. This is the stage where young players become global icons.

The Rose Bowl will pack 90,000 fans into the San Gabriel foothills for a summer evening kickoff. The men's final precedes the women's by one day — both converge on Pasadena to crown champions.

Midday kickoff in late July Pasadena heat. The San Gabriel Mountains frame the north end zone, the sun beats down, and the crowd roar echoes off the arroyo. This is where Olympic legends are crowned.

FEN01.blurb:
First Olympic fencing of the LA28 Games — six and a half hours of nonstop épée and sabre action. Women's épée and men's sabre fields narrow from 64 fencers to the final eight as quarterfinals shape the medal picture.

Épée allows hits anywhere on the body — expect tactical cat-and-mouse exchanges. Sabre is pure speed, full-body attacks with bouts often decided in seconds. Both disciplines feature reigning Paris champions likely to return: Vivian Kong (HKG) in épée, Oh Sang-uk (KOR) in sabre.

The Convention Center's elevated pistes and electronic scoring lights make every touch visible from any seat. Morning sessions tend quieter — serious fans studying technique. By afternoon the knockout drama heats up.
FEN01.potentialContendersIntro: Vivian Kong won Paris 2024 épée gold, Oh Sang-uk took Paris sabre gold — both could appear if they qualify and remain competitive.

Two Olympic champions crowned in one evening. Women's épée and men's sabre semifinals set the medal bracket, then bronze and gold bouts decide the podium under prime-time lights.

Épée's whole-body target zone creates sudden-death drama — a toe touch wins gold. Sabre moves at blur speed with slashing attacks and explosive lunges. Vivian Kong could defend her Paris épée title on home continent; Oh Sang-uk might extend Korea's sabre dynasty.

The Convention Center's dramatic lighting and crowd energy peak for finals nights. Expect cheers in multiple languages as fencing's global community packs the stands.

FEN03.blurb:
Sunday marathon session — men's épée and women's foil from Round of 64 to quarterfinals. Seven-plus hours of fencing as fields compress toward medal contention.

Foil is chess with swords: right-of-way rules reward tactical precision over brute speed. Lee Kiefer seeks a historic third straight Olympic gold for Team USA. Men's épée features full-body targets and patient snipers hunting the winning touch — Paris champion Alexandre Lyssov (CAN) could return.

Convention Center seating wraps the pistes close enough to hear blade clashes and electronic buzzers. Bring patience for the long haul; leave with a doctorate in fencing tactics.

FEN04.blurb:
The night two more Olympic fencing champions are crowned. Men's épée and women's foil semifinals and medal bouts under Convention Center lights, with LA's home crowd potentially lifting Lee Kiefer toward history.

Foil's right-of-way rulebook creates intricate exchanges — referees parse who attacked first in split-second decisions. Kiefer chases a third consecutive Olympic gold, a feat only one woman has achieved in foil. Épée offers no such complications: any legal touch counts, making sudden reversals the norm.

The elevated pistes put you close to the action; electronic scoring flashes red or green the instant a hit registers. Expect a raucous atmosphere if an American reaches the foil final.

FEN05.blurb:
Women's sabre and men's foil collide in a seven-hour knockout gauntlet. Fields shrink from 64 to eight as quarterfinals set the stage for Tuesday's medal rounds.

Sabre is fencing's sprint event — full-speed attacks, slashing cuts, bouts over in heartbeats. France's Manon Apithy-Brunet won Paris sabre gold; Korea's Song Se-ra took silver. Men's foil showcases Ka Long Cheung (HKG), the Paris champion and Tokyo gold medalist, against the world's most precise tacticians.

Convention Center's setup brings you close enough to see sweat and strategy. Early rounds feature unknowns hunting upsets; by afternoon the favorites emerge and the real chess begins.

FEN06.blurb:
The final two individual champions of LA28 fencing are crowned here. Women's sabre and men's foil semifinals and medal bouts bring the individual competitions to a close in prime time.

Foil's tactical rulebook meets sabre's explosive speed in a single session. Ka Long Cheung could cement Hong Kong's foil dynasty; France and Korea typically dominate women's sabre. Medal bouts are best-of-15 touches — long enough for comebacks, short enough for every point to sting.

The Convention Center crowd knows its fencing by now, a week into the Games. Expect knowledgeable cheers for brilliant parries and groans at missed opportunities.

Team fencing amplifies the individual sport's intensity — coaches shout strategy between touches, substitutions shift matchups mid-relay, and comeback victories are common as point deficits evaporate in seconds. The bronze match kicks off the evening before the gold medal showdown.

FFB01.blurb:
Flag football arrives at the Olympics — and the world gets its first look at the sport's biggest stage. This opening session features three games across the men's and women's preliminaries as six-team tournaments begin their march toward history.

The men's side is stacked: if NFL stars like Patrick Mahomes or Tyreek Hill suit up for Team USA (now allowed following a May 2025 league decision), this could be the most talent-dense debut sport in Olympic history. Mexico brings serious challengers who've made flag football a national pastime.

Exposition Park Stadium sets the scene — a college venue that puts you close to the action, where the crack of a pulled flag and the roar of the crowd feel immediate.

The Olympic debut continues under the lights with three more preliminary games. This is flag football's prime-time introduction — a Saturday night showcase for a sport hungry to prove it belongs.

Europe enters the conversation: Austria, Italy, France, and Switzerland are all projected contenders in the men's draw, bringing continental flair to an American-born game. On the women's side, watch for Great Britain, Japan, and Canada to challenge the North American powers.

Exposition Park's intimate setting amplifies every play. The speed is startling, the athleticism undeniable, and the stakes — Olympic qualification on the line — give even early-round games an edge.

The 70-yard field compresses everything — plays unfold in seconds, and five-on-five matchups mean elite athletes are isolated in space with nowhere to hide. If you've only watched tackle football, the pace here is a revelation.

Exposition Park's sightlines make you feel the game's geometry. You'll see route trees develop, watch defensive backs shadow receivers step-for-step, and catch the moment a quarterback's eyes lock onto a target before the flag gets pulled.

Exposition Park's college-venue vibe — tight quarters, vocal fans, bands playing between quarters — gives prelims an energy that bigger stadiums sometimes lack. You're close enough to hear the quarterbacks call plays.

Exposition Park's evening sessions have become the place to be. The venue holds just enough fans to feel packed without feeling anonymous, and the sport's debut status means everyone in the stands knows they're watching history get written.

The final preliminary session before knockouts caps pool play under the lights. After 30 men's matches and a parallel women's slate, the field narrows to four per tournament — and the real drama begins.

This is where matchups get personal. With 12-player rosters and two 20-minute halves on a 70-yard field, you'll see the same athletes matched up again and again. Rivalries develop over 40 minutes, and someone's Olympic dream ends here.

Exposition Park's night atmosphere is electric by now. A week of flag football has turned skeptics into believers, and the crowd has learned to appreciate the sport's nuances — the double-move routes, the zone-coverage switches, the quarterbacks buying time with their feet.

FFB11.blurb:
The men's semifinals — two games, four teams, and only two tickets to the medal rounds. This is where flag football's Olympic debut gets real. Everything before this was prologue.

If Team USA fields NFL athletes, this could be the most talent-stacked semifinal in any Olympic sport. But flag football isn't tackle football — the game rewards different skills, and Mexico, Austria, Italy, France, and Switzerland have all produced world-class specialists who've trained for this format their entire careers.

Exposition Park will be absolutely rocking. The semifinals carry medal implications, and the crowd knows it — these are the games that separate contenders from pretenders on the sport's biggest stage.

This is the session where flag football proves it's not just an American export — it's a global sport with elite athletes from every continent. The two 20-minute halves will fly by, and someone's Olympic dream ends here.

FFB13.blurb:
History gets written: the first-ever Olympic flag football gold medal will be awarded tonight. The bronze-medal game sets the stage; the final delivers the moment the sport has been building toward for decades.

If Team USA reaches the final — and if NFL stars suit up — this could be one of the most-watched debuts in Olympic history. The entire football world will be watching to see if American dominance translates or if a challenger stuns the favorites on home soil.

Exposition Park for a Friday night gold-medal game is an LA28 dream scenario: perfect weather, a sport-savvy crowd, and the kind of atmosphere where every snap feels like it's being carved into Olympic lore. This is why you buy a ticket.

FFB14.blurb:
Flag football's Olympic debut concludes with the women's medal games — and the gold-medal match could be the tournament's most competitive final. Mexico enters ranked No. 1 globally; Team USA brings multi-sport athletes and international dominance; Great Britain, Japan, and Canada have all been projected as threats.

This is the session that determines whether America sweeps both golds or if flag football's global growth produces an upset for the ages. Either way, history is made — the sport's first Olympic champion will be crowned in front of a home crowd.

Exposition Park on a Saturday afternoon, the final day of flag football competition, with a gold medal on the line: the energy will be unmatched. You'll tell people you were there when the sport arrived on the world's biggest stage.

GAR01.blurb:
The opening session of Olympic gymnastics qualification brings the world's top men to DTLA Arena. Watch Team Japan and China's elite all-arounders perform on all six apparatus as they punch their tickets to finals.

Daiki Hashimoto arrives as the reigning two-time Olympic champion, while Zhang Boheng brings the 2024 world title and a hunger to win gold on home continent. The US squad features breakout stars from the 2025 World Championships looking to flip the script after their fifth-place Paris finish.

DTLA Arena transforms into gymnastics central with sightlines built for every rotation. Qualification opens the Olympic stage to all comers — powerhouses and underdogs compete side by side in the same afternoon.

The second men's qualification subdivision delivers another wave of Olympic hopefuls to DTLA Arena. Teams from across the globe rotate through all six apparatus, with scores determining who advances to team, all-around, and apparatus finals.

Japan's reigning Olympic team champions could appear here alongside China's rising stars and American specialists. Stephen Nedoroscik, the pommel horse viral sensation from Paris, may showcase his signature event in pursuit of another final.

Afternoon sun filters into the arena as gymnasts chase precision on rings, vault, and high bar. Qualification rewards consistency — one mistake can cost a final spot, one great routine can launch an Olympic dream.

GAR03.blurb:
Prime time brings the final men's qualification session, closing out the opening weekend of gymnastics. The last subdivision of athletes compete for their Olympic moments under DTLA Arena's lights.

This rotation could feature depth from powerhouse nations filling out their rosters or specialists from smaller federations chasing apparatus final dreams. Every vault, every dismount carries the weight of years of preparation condensed into two and a half hours.

The Saturday night crowd at DTLA Arena creates playoff-level energy for qualification. Watch gymnasts stick landings that will replay in highlight reels all Games long.

GAR04.blurb:
Women's gymnastics arrives with a double-length morning session showcasing two full subdivisions. The US women begin their quest to extend an unprecedented Olympic winning streak on home soil.

Rebeca Andrade, who beat Simone Biles on floor in Paris, headlines the international field. If Biles competes in LA, this is where her Olympic journey begins — qualification on vault, bars, beam, and floor determines everything that follows. Suni Lee and Jordan Chiles bring Olympic pedigree to a stacked American lineup.

DTLA Arena seats 18,000 for gymnastics, and this session will pack them in. Four apparatus, dozens of routines, the first glimpse of who came to LA ready to win.
GAR04.potentialContendersIntro: The reigning Paris floor champion from Brazil is confirmed to contend, while projected US stars could include Olympic veterans if they qualify for the Games.

GAR05.blurb:
Qualification continues Sunday afternoon with a third women's subdivision at DTLA Arena. Nations rotate through vault, bars, beam, and floor as the field for finals takes shape.

The US women compete across multiple subdivisions to spread the depth of their roster. Watch for Brazil's Andrade and emerging stars from Europe and Asia staking their claims to individual final spots. Every tenth matters when qualification margins separate podium dreams from near-misses.

This mid-afternoon window offers an accessible entry to Olympic gymnastics. The format is pure — four events, scores posted live, drama building with every routine.

GAR06.blurb:
The fourth women's subdivision brings Sunday's qualification action into evening at DTLA Arena. Teams and individuals complete their quest for finals advancement as the weekend competition window closes.

This session likely features strong international depth — gymnasts from federations outside the traditional powers hunting apparatus final berths. Qualification is the great equalizer; a clean beam set or explosive vault can come from anywhere.

DTLA Arena at night shifts the atmosphere. Watch gymnasts perform under lights with the Olympic dream fully in view and one shot to grab it.

GAR07.blurb:
The final women's qualification session closes out Sunday night at DTLA Arena — and it's already sold out. The last subdivision of gymnasts take the floor with finals berths on the line.

This could be where Team USA's depth appears, or where international stars make their LA28 debuts. Qualification under the lights carries different stakes than afternoon sessions — the crowd knows these are the final routines before the medal rounds begin.

Prime time Sunday, sold-out arena, Olympic gymnastics. If you have a ticket, you're watching history as the women's field locks into place for the week ahead.

GAR08.blurb:
The men's team final opens the medal rounds with six apparatus of precision and power. Japan defends their Paris gold on American soil, while China and the US chase the podium in front of a raucous home crowd.

Daiki Hashimoto anchors Japan as the world's best all-arounder. Zhang Boheng leads China's charge to reclaim gold after their Paris silver. The US men, fifth in Paris, return with rising talent from the 2025 Worlds aiming to medal at home.

DTLA Arena erupts for team gymnastics — every stuck landing, every tenth of a point, five athletes united. This is Olympic drama distilled: three rotations, three medals, one chance to be the best team in the world.

The most anticipated session of the Olympics: the US women defend their undefeated Olympic team streak on home turf. They haven't lost since 2012. The pressure at DTLA Arena will be seismic.

If Simone Biles competes in LA, this is her signature team moment. Rebeca Andrade leads Brazil's challenge to end the American dynasty. Suni Lee, Jordan Chiles, and Jade Carey could join Biles in a lineup that blends Olympic experience with the weight of national expectation.

Every routine matters across four rotations. The crowd will be deafening, the stakes existential. This is why gymnastics arenas sell out faster than track finals — the team final is ballet, warfare, and theatre in a single night.

Zhang Boheng, the 2025 world champion, stands as Hashimoto's greatest threat. The American contingent looks to end a 16-year US drought in this event, with rising stars like Khoi Young and Asher Hong projected to challenge for medals.

DTLA Arena watches 24 gymnasts compete on floor, pommel horse, rings, vault, parallel bars, and high bar over two and a half hours. The all-around is gymnastics in its purest form — no specialization, no hiding. Just six events and who executes best.

DTLA Arena becomes the center of the sports universe for this session. Every routine carries the weight of legacy — stick your landings here and you're immortal.

Apparatus finals begin with a Saturday morning showcase at DTLA Arena. The top eight qualifiers on select events compete for individual medals in their specialty.

Expect explosive vault finals where gymnasts launch into twisting double layouts, or methodical pommel horse battles decided by hundredths of a point. Stephen Nedoroscik could defend his pommel horse podium from Paris if he qualifies. Carlos Yulo might chase vault or floor repeat gold.

Apparatus finals are gymnastics distilled to its essence — one event, three medals, eight athletes who spent four years perfecting 30 seconds of movement. DTLA Arena's morning light illuminates Olympic dreams realized or deferred.

Monday morning, last apparatus finals, last routines. Gymnasts who've trained a lifetime for 30 seconds get their shot. The medals handed out here close individual careers and launch new ones.

If Simone Biles and a top American male gymnast pair up, the home crowd at DTLA Arena will explode. Brazil could counter with Rebeca Andrade and a rising men's star. Japan's depth on both sides makes them dangerous in any team format.

GLF01.blurb:
The world's best male golfers tee off at Riviera Country Club, one of the most revered courses on the planet. Round 1 sets the tone for 72 holes of stroke play, where world rankings meet Olympic pride.

Riviera has tested PGA Tour pros for decades at the Genesis Invitational — now it hosts Olympic history. The par-4 10th hole is iconic, the Pacific Palisades canyons dramatic, the pressure uniquely Olympic.

This is golf at the highest level on a course that demands precision and nerve. You're watching the opening act of a four-day medal chase in one of the sport's most beautiful settings.
GLF01.potentialContendersIntro: Scottie Scheffler won Paris gold and remains world No. 1 — he's the heavy favorite if he competes. Xander Schauffele won Tokyo gold, Rory McIlroy has been vocal about wanting Olympic glory, and the field will be stacked with major champions.

Round 2 sorts pretenders from contenders. Tee times tighten, leaderboards take shape, and the cut line looms — only the top 60 and ties advance after 36 holes.

Riviera's George C. Thomas Jr. design from 1926 demands strategy and short-game finesse. The canyon walls frame every hole, the greens are slick, the stakes escalate.

You'll see players adjust their games in real time, chasing position or clawing back from a rough start. Round 2 is where Olympic medal hopes begin to crystallize or crack.

Riviera will host the U.S. Men's Open in 2031 — it's a major-championship-caliber test. The par-4 10th, the barranca hazards, the Pacific Palisades light — this is golf at its most dramatic and unforgiving.

Medal Sunday. Every birdie roars through the canyons, every bogey shifts the podium. This is the round that defines Olympic legacies.

A brand-new Olympic event debuts at Riviera: mixed-team four-ball. One man, one woman, representing their country in best-ball format — every birdie counts, and partnerships matter.

Four-ball is aggressive and fast-paced — each player plays their own ball, the better score on each hole counts for the team. It's a different rhythm than individual stroke play, and team chemistry will show.

Riviera's greens and canyon setting make for electric viewing as national prides clash. You're witnessing Olympic history: the first-ever mixed golf medals.

The first Olympic mixed-team golf medals will be decided in foursomes format — alternate shot, maximum pressure. One ball, two players, total trust required.

Foursomes is the purest test of partnership: you hit, your partner hits, mistakes are shared, birdies are euphoric. It's a format that strips away individual safety nets and demands flawless teamwork.

Riviera's iconic holes — the par-4 10th, the closing stretch — will test nerve and chemistry under Olympic stakes. History will be made Monday morning in the Palisades canyons.

GRY01.blurb:
The first wave of rhythmic gymnastics qualification unfolds across four apparatus — hoop, ball, clubs, and ribbon. Watch gymnasts blend balletic grace with split-second tosses and catches, chasing the top 24 spots for Friday's final.

Germany's Darja Varfolomeev, the Paris all-around champion and reigning world titlist, is projected to compete if she qualifies for LA28. Italy's Sofia Raffaeli, a Paris bronze medalist and multi-time world champion, could also appear. Expect emerging stars from traditional powerhouses and lesser-known contenders seizing their Olympic moment.

Galen Center's intimate sightlines put you close to the ribbon spirals and hoop rolls. The chalk dust, the apparatus clatter, the anxious coaches — it's where Olympic dreams begin on a blue carpet.

GRY03.blurb:
Five gymnasts, one apparatus set, perfect synchronization required. The group all-around qualification is rhythmic gymnastics as ensemble theater — ribbons intertwining mid-flight, hoops exchanged in geometric patterns.

Israel's group won gold in Paris and could return as favorites if they qualify for LA28. Traditional powers like Italy, Bulgaria, and China are likely to field competitive ensembles. Watch for split-second timing as gymnasts trade apparatus without breaking formation or tempo.

Galen Center becomes a geometry classroom come to life. The swish of five ribbons in unison, the collective gasp when a toss nearly collides — it's mesmerizing chaos that somehow resolves into art.

GRY04.blurb:
The individual all-around final crowns rhythmic gymnastics' Olympic champion across four apparatus routines. It's ballet, circus, and sport compressed into ten minutes per gymnast — where a ribbon never touches the floor and artistry scores as heavily as difficulty.

Darja Varfolomeev could defend her Paris title if she qualifies and competes for Germany. Sofia Raffaeli, Italy's apparatus virtuoso and Paris bronze winner, is expected to contend. Expect the 24 best gymnasts in the world executing tosses that hang impossibly long and dance sequences that blur athleticism and performance art.

Galen Center's Friday night crowd will erupt for perfect ribbon spirals and gasp at bobbled catches. Medal stakes, dramatic music, sequined leotards catching the lights — this is rhythmic gymnastics at its most theatrical.

GRY05.blurb:
The group all-around final is rhythmic gymnastics' ultimate ensemble piece. Five gymnasts perform two routines with different apparatus sets, choreographed to the millimeter — a single mistimed exchange can cost a medal.

Israel's Paris gold-winning group is projected to be the favorite if they qualify for LA28. Italy, Bulgaria, China, and other traditional powers could field teams capable of medaling. Watch for the impossible — five hoops airborne simultaneously, ribbon tosses that cross paths without tangling, formations that morph seamlessly from circle to line.

Galen Center on Saturday afternoon, with medals on the line and five gymnasts moving as one organism. When it works, it's hypnotic. When apparatus collide mid-air, it's heartbreak in sequins.

GTR01.blurb:
Watch athletes launch 30 feet into the air at DTLA Arena, performing ten consecutive skills that blend somersaults, twists, and split-second timing. The men's final crowns the world's most fearless gymnast — the one who can execute the highest difficulty routine without a single bobble.

Qualification runs in the afternoon, then the top eight return for the medal round. Scoring rewards difficulty, execution, time of flight, and staying centered on the trampoline — one wandering bounce can cost a podium spot.

DTLA Arena's intimate sightlines put you close to the action. You'll hear the rhythmic thump of the bed, feel the crowd's collective gasp when an athlete rotates through a triple twisting triple somersault, and watch physics-defying flight in real time.

GTR02.blurb:
The evening session brings women's trampoline to DTLA Arena — ten bounces, maximum airtime, technical precision measured to the tenth of a point. Athletes soar three stories high, rotating through skills most people couldn't imagine attempting on solid ground.

Qualification narrows the field to eight finalists, who then perform one last routine for Olympic medals. Execution deductions are ruthless — judges penalize wandering on the bed, form breaks mid-air, and anything less than a controlled landing.

Bryony Page won Paris gold for Britain in this event. If she qualifies for LA28, expect the arena to erupt when her name is announced — though the field won't be set until 2027 World Championships and World Cup results are in.

The arena's steep seating gives everyone a clear view of the athletes' full flight path. You'll see the concentration on their faces before launch, then watch them disappear into the rafters and snap through impossibly fast rotations before sticking the landing.

JUD10.blurb:
Women's -70kg and Men's -90kg crown their Olympic champions. The Men's -90kg final carries heightened anticipation — this is Lasha Bekauri's weight class from his Tokyo gold run, and Georgia's judo tradition makes them perennial medal threats here.

Repechage battles for bronze open the session before semifinals narrow the field to four. Then the finals deliver Olympic immortality to two athletes. Japan and France could both field medal contenders across both divisions based on recent international results.

The LA Convention Center hosted the Main Press Headquarters during the 1984 Games — now it hosts the competition itself. Two mats, two finals, the weight of Olympic history pressing down on every throw.
JUD10.potentialContendersIntro: Men's -90kg could feature Georgian and Japanese medal threats, while Women's -70kg may draw strong French and Japanese contenders.

JUD11.blurb:
The heavyweight divisions arrive with Men's -100kg and Women's -78kg elimination rounds. These weight classes showcase judo's power game — bigger athletes throwing with explosive force, matches decided by moments of overwhelming kinetic energy.

Elimination rounds run for four hours across two mats, cutting the field down to semifinalists. France could bring strength to both divisions, while Japan remains competitive even in the heavier classes. Women's -78kg borders on the true heavyweight category, blending technique with raw power.

Watch the tatami mats absorb harder impacts as the weight classes climb. The converted convention space trembles slightly when a 100kg athlete gets thrown for ippon — that's Olympic judo at its most visceral.
JUD11.potentialContendersIntro: The heavier weight classes could see strong French and Japanese entries, with multiple nations competitive in both divisions.

JUD12.blurb:
Men's -100kg and Women's -78kg reach their medal rounds. The heavyweight-adjacent classes deliver explosive power judo — athletes who can end matches with a single perfectly timed throw, but also possess the technical mastery to grind through four-minute tactical battles.

Repechage offers bronze-medal paths before semifinals set the final matchups. Four medals will be awarded across two weight classes. France could contend strongly in both divisions based on their consistent heavyweight depth at recent Olympics.

The evening session brings LA Convention Center Hall 2 to full intensity. Two champions crowned, two national anthems, the smell of tatami mats and competition sweat — this is Olympic judo stripped to its essential drama.
JUD12.potentialContendersIntro: The heavier weight classes may see French and Japanese medal contenders, with competitive depth across multiple nations.

JUD13.blurb:
The tournament's final individual weight classes begin with Women's +78kg and Men's +100kg elimination rounds. This is heavyweight judo — the biggest, strongest athletes in the sport throwing with force that makes the mats shake and the crowd gasp.

Men's +100kg is Teddy Riner's weight class. The four-time Olympic champion won Paris gold and has confirmed LA28 as his final Games before retirement — if he competes, this session could feature the sport's greatest athlete. Women's +78kg saw Iraq's Yasmin Al-Obaidi win Paris gold in an upset.

The convention center hosts judo's most physically imposing competitors. These are elimination rounds, so the star names share mat time with unknown qualifiers hunting upsets — David-versus-Goliath matchups that define Olympic judo's appeal.
JUD13.potentialContendersIntro: Men's +100kg could feature Teddy Riner in his final Olympic appearance, while Women's +78kg may see the Paris 2024 medalists return.

JUD14.blurb:
The final individual Olympic judo medals will be decided in this session. Women's +78kg and Men's +100kg reach their conclusions, and the Men's heavyweight final could be Teddy Riner's Olympic farewell — the French legend won Paris gold and has announced LA28 as his last Games.

Repechage bronze battles open the session before semifinals set the championship matchups. Then two finals crown two champions, including potentially the greatest judoka ever in his final Olympic moment. Yasmin Al-Obaidi upset the field to win Paris gold in Women's +78kg; France's Romane Dicko could challenge strongly.

LA Convention Center Hall 2 will reach peak atmosphere if Riner competes. Four-time Olympic champion, confirmed final appearance, competing on American soil — this could be one of the defining moments of LA28 judo.

JUD15.blurb:
The Mixed Team event brings judo's final act — nations fielding six athletes (three men, three women) in a winner-take-all elimination bracket. Teams face off in individual bouts across weight classes; first to four victories advances. It's pure drama condensed into a single day.

Elimination rounds, repechage, and semifinals all happen in one marathon session. This format debuted at Tokyo and delivers edge-of-seat tension — a single upset bout can swing an entire team match. France, Japan, and Georgia could all field medal-contending squads based on their individual depth.

LA Convention Center Hall 2 transforms into a team battleground. National coaches managing six athletes, strategy shifting bout-to-bout, entire delegations riding on individual throws — this is judo as team sport, and it's electric.
JUD15.potentialContendersIntro: The Mixed Team event could see strong squads from Japan, France, and Georgia based on their depth across individual weight classes.

JUD16.blurb:
The mixed team event is judo's ultimate team showdown — six weight classes (three men's, three women's), head-to-head bouts, winner-take-all stakes. Each team sends one judoka per class; win four and you claim the medal.

Japan swept gold at both Tokyo and Paris in this format and will likely enter as favorites again if they qualify. France, bronze in Tokyo and silver in Paris on home soil, could field Teddy Riner in his final Olympic appearance — a potential farewell on the mat for the sport's most decorated heavyweight. Georgia's deep roster across men's divisions makes them perennial contenders for a podium spot.

The LA Convention Center's multi-mat setup means warmups and early rounds happen simultaneously nearby, but finals consolidate on a single mat with the full crowd locked in. Judo moves fast — a single ippon can flip momentum — and the team format turns every match into a pressure cooker.
JUD16.potentialContendersIntro: The final team rosters won't be set until closer to the Games, but recent results suggest these powerhouses will be in the medal hunt.

LAC01.blurb:
Lacrosse returns to the Olympics after a 120-year absence, and the opening session throws you straight into the chaos. Three men's games in rapid succession introduce the sixes format — six players, four eight-minute quarters, end-to-end sprints and goals coming in bunches.

The six-team field is still taking shape through 2026-2027 qualifying, but USA and Canada are locks, and the Haudenosaunee — the Iroquois Nationals, representing the Indigenous nations who invented the sport — are expected to compete in a moment of profound historical significance. Round-robin play means every game counts toward medal seeding.

Exposition Park Stadium hosts all lacrosse action, a compact venue that keeps you close to the athleticism and lets the sport's intensity land.
LAC01.potentialContendersIntro: The men's field is still being finalized through 2027 World Championships qualifying, but three teams are projected frontrunners based on world rankings and automatic host entry.

potentialContenders.note: Dominant force in world lacrosse; automatic host qualification expected

LAC02.blurb:
The evening session shifts to women's lacrosse, making its Olympic debut. Three qualifying games unfold under lights as six teams begin their medal pursuit in a round-robin format that rewards every win.

USA women are the reigning world champions in sixes and overwhelming favorites if they qualify as expected through the host pathway. The field is still being determined via continental championships and the 2027 World Lacrosse Sixes Championships, but the American squad toured this very venue in 2024 and know the sightlines cold.

Exposition Park puts you in the mix — close quarters, tight angles, nowhere to hide when a goal horn sounds.

LAC03.blurb:
Day two of round-robin play sharpens the picture. Three more men's games test depth and conditioning as teams fight for position in the medal bracket, with the top four advancing to semifinals.

USA and Canada could face off in this window if the schedule breaks that way — a rivalry that's defined North American lacrosse for generations. The Haudenosaunee bring a different intensity: for them, this is reclaiming space in a sport they gave the world. Sixes format accelerates everything — substitutions on the fly, possessions measured in seconds.

Exposition Park keeps the energy bottled up and loud. Midday sun, compact stands, players close enough to hear the chatter.

USA women are the team to beat if they qualify as projected, but the field expands as qualifying unfolds through 2027. The top 100 women's programs worldwide are eligible to enter the Olympic pathway, and continental champions will bring regional pride and tactical wrinkles.

The stadium's compact footprint means you're inside the moment, not observing from a distance. It's loud, it's fast, and the ball rarely stops moving.

Midweek, midday, halfway through the round-robin. Teams are managing bodies, adjusting tactics, and jockeying for playoff position as the medal bracket takes shape.

USA-Canada is the marquee rivalry if both appear in this session — the sport's oldest grudge match, played at Olympic stakes for the first time in over a century. The Haudenosaunee add a layer of meaning beyond medals: lacrosse as cultural reclamation, played on the world's biggest stage. Sixes keeps the tempo relentless — no time to settle, no margin for lapses.

Exposition Park offers no frills, just proximity and atmosphere. The game happens right in front of you.

Sixes rewards fast breaks and quick thinking; one hot quarter can flip a game.

Exposition Park's setup keeps you locked in. Tight venue, big crowd energy, every shot audible off the crossbar.

USA and Canada are expected to have already punched their tickets by now, but the fight for third and fourth could come down to goal differential or head-to-head results. The Haudenosaunee carry symbolic weight, but they're not a ceremonial entry — they're competitors. Sixes doesn't allow coasting; every shift demands full effort.

Exposition Park channels the noise and heat back onto the field. It's an intimate Olympic experience for a sport reclaiming its place.

Continental champions bring different styles — European possession, North American pace, emerging programs with nothing to lose. Sixes rewards risk-taking, and teams trailing in the standings might gamble everything in this session.

Exposition Park compresses the action into a tight bowl. You're close enough to see strategy unfold in real time.

The Haudenosaunee could be playing for a medal-round berth or pride, depending on how the week has unfolded. Sixes guarantees nothing drags; teams empty the tank.

LAC11.blurb:
Lacrosse crowns its first Olympic men's champions in 120 years. Bronze medal game opens the session, gold closes it — two games, two podiums, and a century of absence erased.

USA and Canada are overwhelming favorites to meet in the gold medal game, renewing the sport's fiercest rivalry on the biggest stage it's ever had. The Haudenosaunee could compete for bronze, adding a layer of cultural resonance to the medal stand. Sixes accelerates every Olympic emotion — speed, stakes, and no room to breathe.

Exposition Park hosts the sport's rebirth. It's not SoFi Stadium grandeur, but it's intimate, loud, and perfectly suited to lacrosse's intensity.

Women's lacrosse makes its Olympic debut complete with a Saturday night doubleheader under the lights — bronze first, then gold. This is the sport's arrival, not just its return.

USA women are overwhelming favorites if they qualify as expected, likely facing a continental champion or world championship qualifier in the gold medal game. The bronze game offers a different kind of drama — teams that came close, fighting for a place on the podium in the sport's first-ever Olympic medal round.

Exposition Park closes the lacrosse tournament where it started. Tight venue, big moment, history being written in real time.

MPN01.blurb:
The semifinal winnows the field for LA28's first-ever modern pentathlon with obstacle racing. Fencers, swimmers, and endurance athletes collide in a four-and-a-half-hour test spanning épée bouts, 200m freestyle, the new eight-obstacle gauntlet, and a laser-run chase.

The obstacle course—60 to 70 meters of walls, rings, and monkey bars—replaces the old horse-riding discipline, turning head-to-head races into instant drama. Every second converts to three points, so a stumble on the rings can erase a fencing lead. The laser-run finish fuses the final standings: 3.2km run, four shooting stops, five laser-pistol targets per round or time penalties.

Valley Complex 2 near Sepulveda Basin hosts the compressed format—Paris 2024 finals ran 90 minutes, and the semifinal keeps that breakneck pace. You'll watch athletes rotate through stations, each discipline flipping the leaderboard before the run-shoot sprint decides who advances.
MPN01.potentialContendersIntro: Michelle Gulyas won Paris 2024 gold, but no LA28 rosters are set. Expect a mix of European powerhouses and emerging obstacle specialists when qualifiers are finalized.

potentialContenders.name: Michelle Gulyas
potentialContenders.note: Paris 2024 gold medalist; could defend if she qualifies for LA28

potentialContenders.note: Hungary, Great Britain, and France traditionally field strong women's fields

MPN02.blurb:
One morning, four disciplines, and a field chasing Olympic final spots. The men's semifinal opens with fencing ranking rounds—épée bouts that seed the obstacle course heats—then pivots to 200m freestyle swimming before the new 70-meter obstacle gauntlet debuts under the San Fernando Valley sun.

The obstacle course is untested at Olympic scale: six fixed challenges plus two randomized, head-to-head races where a slip on the monkey bars costs three points per second. Ahmed Elgendy dominated this format at Paris, but the LA28 course design and athlete field remain unknown. The laser-run—3km with four shooting stops, five laser-pistol hits required each round—closes the session and sets final seeding.

Sepulveda Basin's sprawling venue allows spectators to track athletes station-to-station as the leaderboard reshuffles. The compressed schedule means constant movement: one event flows into the next, no downtime, the semifinal pressure visible in every transition.
MPN02.potentialContendersIntro: Ahmed Elgendy won Paris gold in the debut of obstacle racing, but LA28 qualification won't close until early 2028. Likely contenders include recent world medalists and athletes with obstacle-course racing backgrounds.

MPN03.blurb:
Modern pentathlon crowns its Olympic champion in two hours flat. Fencing opens with round-robin épée bouts that establish the starting point spread. Swimmers race 200m freestyle for time-converted points, then the field sprints the 60-meter obstacle course—walls, rings, monkey bars—in head-to-head heats where every second equals three points and one stumble can sink a medal chase.

The laser-run decides gold: athletes start staggered by cumulative points, so whoever crosses the finish line first wins outright. Four shooting stops along 3km, five laser-pistol targets per round, time penalties for misses. The Paris final saw dramatic shifts in the final kilometer as leaders missed targets and chasers closed gaps.

Valley Complex 2 stages the ultimate all-rounder test—no single discipline dominates, no lead is safe. The crowd tracks athletes rotating through stations, the leaderboard live and volatile, until the laser-run sends them onto the course and out of sight for the final sprint home.
MPN03.potentialContendersIntro: Michelle Gulyas won Paris 2024, but LA28 rosters won't be confirmed until qualification wraps in early 2028. Expect a field mixing European pentathlon powers with athletes who've mastered the new obstacle format.

MPN04.blurb:
Five disciplines, one day, one Olympic champion. The men's final compresses fencing, swimming, obstacle course, and laser-run into a two-hour sprint where no lead is safe and the leaderboard flips with every event. Fencing ranking rounds seed the field, 200m freestyle adds time-converted points, then the 70-meter obstacle gauntlet—walls, rings, monkey bars—turns the standings upside down in head-to-head heats.

The laser-run finish is modern pentathlon's signature chaos: athletes start staggered by cumulative points, so the first across the line wins gold. 3km run, four shooting stops, five laser-pistol targets per round or face time penalties. Ahmed Elgendy won Paris with a late surge; the LA28 course design and field could produce even tighter margins.

Valley Complex 2 lets you watch the drama unfold station by station—athletes rotating through disciplines, the leaderboard live and shifting, until the laser-run sends them out of sight for the final race. The finish line determines the podium, no calculations needed, whoever runs and shoots best when it counts takes home gold.

MTB01.blurb:
The men's mountain bike final is an all-out test of power and precision across 90 minutes of racing. Riders tackle a 4-6 km circuit of rock gardens, steep climbs, and technical descents through the hills east of LA, repeating laps until one emerges with gold.

Qualification runs through May 2028 via UCI rankings, so rosters won't be set until weeks before the Games. Tokyo gold medalist Tom Pidcock could return if he qualifies, bringing his signature combination of road fitness and bike-handling wizardry.

The Industry Hills course sits adjacent to Pacific Palms Resort, purpose-built into natural terrain between 550 and 750 feet of elevation. Spectator zones line the twisting track — expect California heat, dust plumes on descents, and the metallic clatter of bikes hitting rock at speed.
MTB01.potentialContendersIntro: The field will be determined by UCI rankings through late May 2028, with Tokyo's podium and recent World Cup form offering clues to who might qualify.

potentialContenders.name: Tom Pidcock
potentialContenders.note: Tokyo 2020 gold medalist; multi-discipline star if he qualifies for LA28

MTB02.blurb:
One day after the men, the women's cross-country final brings the same brutal course and medal stakes. Ninety minutes of racing on a technical loop through the San Gabriel Valley hills — climbs that shred the lungs, descents that demand split-second line choices.

Pauline Ferrand-Prevot dominated on home soil in Paris, but whether she'll race LA28 remains uncertain as qualification extends into May 2028. The women's field has been incredibly strong in recent years, and whoever qualifies will face the same heat and dust that defines California mountain biking.

Industry Hills offers close-up viewing as riders navigate drops, jumps, and twisting terrain between Pacific Palms Resort and the surrounding ridges. You'll hear the rhythm of shifting gears, see rooster tails of dirt, feel the crowd energy spike on the decisive climbs.
MTB02.potentialContendersIntro: Qualification won't be finalized until late May 2028, but Paris 2024 results and World Cup standings suggest likely contenders.

potentialContenders.note: Paris 2024 gold medalist; participation at LA28 not yet confirmed

OWS01.blurb:
The women's 10km marathon swim brings nearly two hours of ocean racing to the Pacific off Long Beach. Twenty-two swimmers navigate currents, swells, and each other in a test of endurance and tactics that often ends in a sprint finish separated by hundredths of a second.

Unlike pool racing, open water demands constant adaptation—reading the ocean, drafting off competitors, timing your move. Wetsuits may be allowed depending on water temperature, adding another variable to strategy.

Belmont Shore offers a rare chance to watch Olympic swimming from the beach. You'll see the field make the turn buoys and charge toward shore, with the finish visible from the sand as athletes surge out of the Pacific.
OWS01.potentialContendersIntro: Sharon van Rouwendaal won both Rio 2016 and Paris 2024, making her the most decorated Olympic open-water swimmer. Qualification begins at the 2027 World Championships, so the LA28 field won't be set until spring 2028.

potentialContenders.name: Sharon van Rouwendaal
potentialContenders.note: Two-time Olympic champion (2016, 2024); could become the first three-peat winner in open water history if she competes

potentialContenders.name: Moesha Johnson
potentialContenders.note: Silver medalist at Paris 2024; strong in ocean conditions

potentialContenders.name: Ginevra Taddeucci
potentialContenders.note: Bronze medalist at Paris 2024

OWS02.blurb:
The men's 10km marathon swim closes two hours of racing with a finish that could be decided by a fingertip. Swimmers battle Pacific currents and each other, jockeying for position before a final sprint that turns marathon endurance into a pure speed test.

Navigation matters as much as fitness—drift off course and you add precious meters. Drafting behind rivals saves energy but requires reading the pack and timing your break.

The finish comes straight into shore at Belmont Beach, where you'll watch swimmers emerge from the ocean after 10 kilometers of open water. Long Beach has hosted national and Pan Pacific open water championships, bringing that experience to the Olympic stage.
OWS02.potentialContendersIntro: Kristof Rasovszky dominated the Paris 2024 men's 10km to take gold. The LA28 field will be determined through 2027 World Championships qualification and a spring 2028 qualifier, so rosters remain projected.

RCB01.blurb:
Coastal rowing makes its Olympic debut at Belmont Shore, where the Pacific meets the sport in its wildest form. Athletes sprint across the beach, leap into open-hulled boats, and row 250 meters out through waves and swells before racing back to shore.

This morning session features time trials and the first elimination round for both men's and women's solo events. Expect chaotic starts as rowers wrestle with currents and wind — it's as much seamanship as raw power.

Belmont Shore's break wall offers some wave protection, but conditions remain unpredictable. You'll watch from the beach as athletes battle elements that can upend even the strongest rowers in seconds.
RCB01.potentialContendersIntro: Mediterranean nations with strong coastal traditions — Italy, Croatia, and France — are projected to field competitive entries, though no rosters have been announced yet.

potentialContenders.name: Italy
potentialContenders.note: Strong coastal rowing tradition from Mediterranean competition; likely to field experienced solo rowers

potentialContenders.name: Croatia
potentialContenders.note: Established coastal program; could contend in both men's and women's solo events

potentialContenders.name: France
potentialContenders.note: Mediterranean coastal rowing experience; projected to qualify competitive athletes

RCB02.blurb:
The first-ever Olympic medals in coastal rowing will be decided in the Pacific surf. Eight finalists in each gender sprint, row through waves, and sprint again — the format rewards explosive beach speed and fearless boat handling equally.

The 250-meter course runs perpendicular to shore through open water. Wind shifts and wave sets can change the race in an instant, making strategy as critical as fitness.

Belmont Shore puts you right on the sand where the action starts and finishes. The beach sprint finishes are pure theater — rowers stumbling through surf, legs burning, lungs screaming for the line.
RCB02.potentialContendersIntro: Italy, Croatia, and France are expected to challenge for the podium based on their coastal rowing programs, though qualification is still ahead.

potentialContenders.name: Italy
potentialContenders.note: Mediterranean coastal strength; likely to field medal contenders in both men's and women's finals

potentialContenders.name: Croatia
potentialContenders.note: Established in open-water racing; could appear in both finals if they qualify

potentialContenders.name: France
potentialContenders.note: Strong coastal tradition; projected to compete for medals if they advance

RCB03.blurb:
The mixed double sculls makes its Olympic debut in the surf. Two athletes per boat — one woman, one man — coordinate their beach sprint, navigate the waves together, and sprint back in sync.

Time trials set the seeding for the knockout round that follows immediately. Sixteen crews will be whittled down as the Pacific tests their teamwork and nerve in equal measure.

The mixed format adds tactical wrinkles — crew weight distribution, synchronized transitions from sand to water. Watch for pairs that move as a single unit through the chaos.
RCB03.potentialContendersIntro: Nations with coastal rowing programs — Italy, Croatia, and France among them — are likely to field competitive mixed pairs, though no crews have been announced.

potentialContenders.name: Italy
potentialContenders.note: Mediterranean coastal experience; expected to field a strong mixed double if they qualify

potentialContenders.name: Croatia
potentialContenders.note: Coastal tradition; could contend in mixed format with experienced crews

potentialContenders.name: France
potentialContenders.note: Established program; projected to compete in mixed doubles based on past coastal results

RCB04.blurb:
History gets made in Long Beach as the first Olympic gold medal in coastal rowing is awarded to a mixed double sculls crew. Eight pairs remain after the morning's eliminations — this final decides who mastered the Pacific.

The mixed format demands perfect synchronization through every phase: the beach sprint launch, the pull through swells, the frantic return to shore. One stumble, one mistimed stroke in the waves, and the medal slips away.

Belmont Shore delivers an intimate venue where you'll hear the athletes breathing and see every wave that reshapes the race. The beach finish — both rowers sprinting together — is unlike anything else at the Games.
RCB04.potentialContendersIntro: Italy, Croatia, and France have the coastal expertise to contend for the inaugural gold, though qualification will determine the final field.

ROW01.blurb:
The 2028 rowing program opens on flat water in Long Beach, returning to the first man-made rowing course in the United States. Three events kick off the Olympic regatta — women's single sculls, men's double sculls, and men's pair — each sending boats through their opening heats.

Prelims are the accessible entry point: fields are wide open, nobody's eliminated yet, and you're watching rowers lay down their markers for the week ahead. These heats determine who advances directly and who must fight through repechages.

Marine Stadium sits just off the Long Beach skyline, and summer mornings on the water are California at its best. The 2,000-meter course is straight and sheltered, built for the 1932 Games and back nearly a century later.

ROW02.blurb:
The second morning mixes heats and semifinals across five events — men's single sculls, women's double sculls and pair heats, plus men's double sculls and pair semis. The field is thinning; semifinalists are racing for lane assignments in finals, while heats are still sorting qualifiers from repechage-bound boats.

Single sculls is rowing's loneliest discipline — one rower, two oars, no crew to lean on. Doubles and pairs demand synchronization; the smallest timing error costs speed. Semifinals carry pressure — a bad race means you're racing for bronze at best.

Marine Stadium's flat water rewards technical precision. Early Sunday sessions draw the local rowing community; you'll see juniors and masters watching the world's best on the same course where they train.

ROW03.blurb:
Monday brings the first Olympic rowing medals of LA28 — men's double sculls and pair A finals, with B and C finals preceding them. Women's single sculls quarterfinals and women's double sculls and pair semifinals fill out the session, setting the stage for their medal races later in the week.

The men's pair final is a showcase for New Zealand's elite rowing program, which has historically dominated the event despite the country's small population. The double sculls final rewards endurance and boat speed over 2,000 meters of racing.

Marine Stadium's sightlines make every stroke visible from the grandstand. The harbor setting and California light give the venue a distinctly West Coast feel — less formal than European rowing strongholds, but just as serious about the racing.
ROW03.potentialContendersIntro: New Zealand's pair program has historically set the standard in the event, though LA28 rosters are not yet confirmed.

ROW04.blurb:
Tuesday afternoon sessions deliver the first women's medals — double sculls and pair A finals — alongside men's quad sculls and four heats and men's single sculls quarterfinals. The women's finals close out the small boat events; quad and four heats open the big boat program.

Women's pair and double sculls finals reward the week's fastest qualifiers with medal lanes. Heats in the quad (four scullers) and four (sweep rowers, one oar each) introduce the power events — bigger boats, more rowers, and the tactics of crew racing.

Afternoon racing at Marine Stadium means warmer conditions and the Long Beach skyline sharpening in the afternoon light. The venue's history runs deep — 1932 Olympians raced this same stretch of harbor nearly a century ago.

ROW05.blurb:
An early-morning session starting at 8 a.m. — the coolest, calmest conditions of the regatta. Women's quad sculls and four heats open the program, followed by women's single sculls semifinals and E/F finals, then men's quad and four A and B finals for medals.

The men's quad and four finals are big-boat power racing — four rowers per boat, maximum crew weight, and the synchronization that defines sweep rowing. Women's single sculls semifinals narrow the field to six finalists; E and F finals determine final rankings for non-medalists.

Sunrise sessions at Marine Stadium are a Southern California tradition. The water is flat, the harbor is quiet, and the rowers are racing in prime conditions before the day heats up.

ROW06.blurb:
Thursday morning features men's eight heats — rowing's premier event, eight rowers plus a coxswain. Women's quad sculls and four A and B finals award medals, and men's single sculls wraps semifinals and E/F finals.

The men's eight heats are the first look at the marquee boats. Great Britain has been a perennial contender in the event, bringing depth and a storied rowing tradition. Eight rowers moving in perfect rhythm generate brutal boat speed; the eight is rowing's most iconic race.

Marine Stadium's 2,000-meter straight course is ideal for eights racing — no turns, no chop, just flat water and a clean view from start to finish. The local rowing community turns out for eights heats; it's the event that defines Olympic rowing.
ROW06.potentialContendersIntro: Great Britain has historically been a powerhouse in the men's eight, though LA28 lineups are not yet finalized.

ROW07.blurb:
Friday morning at Marine Stadium: women's eight heats, women's single sculls A/B/C/D finals, and the men's eight A and B finals. The men's eight final is the day's centerpiece — the Olympic regatta's showcase race, eight rowers and a cox in a 2,000-meter sprint for gold.

Great Britain's men's eight is likely to contend if they qualify, continuing a tradition of British dominance in the event. The A final is six minutes of synchronized power; the rhythm of eight oars, the cox calling the race, and the crowd watching one of sport's great endurance tests.

Women's single sculls finals distribute medals across four races, rewarding a week of qualifying heats, repechages, and semifinals. Marine Stadium's grandstand offers clear sightlines down the entire course — you'll see the finish and hear the race call echoing across the harbor.
ROW07.potentialContendersIntro: Great Britain is expected to field a strong men's eight if they qualify, continuing their tradition in the event.

ROW08.blurb:
The Olympic rowing regatta closes Saturday morning with the women's eight A and B finals and men's single sculls A/B/C/D finals. The women's eight final is the last race of the session — eight rowers, one cox, and the final medals awarded at Marine Stadium.

Romania won gold in the women's eight at Paris 2024 and could return as a contender if they qualify for LA28. The eight is a test of endurance, synchronization, and raw crew power; the A final determines the Olympic champions in rowing's most demanding boat class.

Marine Stadium returns to Olympic competition for the first time since 1932, closing the regatta where American rowing history began. The harbor, the skyline, and the California morning light make the venue feel distinct from Europe's traditional rowing lakes.
ROW08.potentialContendersIntro: Romania won gold in the women's eight at Paris 2024 and is projected to contend again if they qualify for LA28.

RU701.blurb:
Six back-to-back pool matches kick off women's rugby sevens at Carson Stadium, where 12 teams chase quarterfinal spots over three days of round-robin play. Each 14-minute contest is a sprint — speed, skill, and stamina tested across two seven-minute halves on a full-size pitch.

New Zealand enters as reigning Olympic champion from Paris 2024, while the USA women will lean into deafening home-crowd energy in Carson. Pool positions set the knockout bracket; early upsets can reshape medal paths before the week is out.

Carson Stadium's 27,000 seats wrap tight around the field, creating an intimate sevens festival atmosphere. The compact venue has hosted World Rugby Sevens Series stops before — expect drums, chants, and a carnival vibe from opening whistle to final conversion.
RU701.potentialContendersIntro: Pool play features the world's top-ranked sevens nations, led by New Zealand's defending Olympic champions and a charged-up USA squad playing at home.

potentialContenders.note: Reigning Olympic champions, Paris 2024 gold medalists

RU702.blurb:
The evening slate brings six more pool matches under lights, closing out Day 1 of women's sevens. Teams that won in the morning session look to double up; losers face must-win pressure.

Fourteen minutes can swing a tournament — a single missed tackle or breakaway try shifts the entire bracket. The USA women, fourth in Paris, could face a marquee pool opponent here with 27,000 voices behind them.

Night sevens at Carson Stadium has a different energy: cooler air, brighter lights, louder crowds. The compact bowl keeps every cheer and groan on the field, amplifying the stakes of each pool result.
RU702.potentialContendersIntro: Evening pool matches could feature clashes between top-seeded teams, with New Zealand and the USA among those expected to compete for early leads.

RU703.blurb:
Day 2 brings the final pool matches, with quarterfinal seeding on the line. Teams with 2-0 records can lock top spots; those at 0-2 fight to avoid the classification bracket.

New Zealand and the USA will both look to finish pool play undefeated, setting up favorable knockout matchups. A surprise loss here could mean facing a medal favorite one round earlier than planned.

Carson Stadium's morning sessions have a festival-like buildup — fans filtering in, teams warming up within earshot of the stands. By the third match, the crowd is fully engaged and the stakes are clear.

RU704.blurb:
Quarterfinals separate medal contenders from the rest — four matches, four teams punched through to medal rounds. The top eight from pool play clash in knockout rugby; one loss and you're battling for fifth, not gold.

New Zealand will be heavily favored if they reach this stage, but sevens is ruthless — fatigue, injuries, and momentum swings can undo favorites in 14 minutes. The USA, buoyed by home noise, could meet a European or Pacific power here.

Carson Stadium's evening sessions ratchet up the intensity. Quarterfinals draw the loudest crowds, the tightest margins, and the sport's signature chaos — end-to-end runs, last-second tackles, sudden-death stakes.
RU704.potentialContendersIntro: Quarterfinals will likely feature New Zealand, the USA, and Australia among the top seeds, with pool runners-up creating potential upset matchups.

RU705.blurb:
Semifinal day decides the medal matchups — two matches, four teams, two guaranteed podium spots. Win and you're playing for gold; lose and it's a bronze-medal consolation in primetime.

If New Zealand and the USA both advance, a semifinal meeting would be electric — defending champions versus a home nation desperate to reach the final. Placement matches for fifth through eighth also carry pride and Olympic legacy.

Afternoon sevens at Carson Stadium means full sun, full stands, and maximum energy. The compact venue funnels crowd noise directly onto the pitch, making every ruck and breakdown feel like it's happening in a cauldron.
RU705.potentialContendersIntro: Semifinals are projected to feature New Zealand, the USA, and Australia if seeding holds, with one European or Pacific qualifier rounding out the final four.

RU706.blurb:
The women's gold medal match crowns Olympic champions under lights at Carson Stadium. New Zealand won in Paris; if they return to the final, they'll face a fired-up opponent — possibly the USA in front of a home crowd — gunning to dethrone them.

Sevens finals are 14 minutes of full-throttle rugby: no timeouts, no substitutions mid-half, just speed and skill under maximum pressure. The bronze match beforehand carries its own weight — third place at the Olympics is still a podium and a lifetime memory.

Evening finals at Carson bring festival peaks: packed stands, championship stakes, and the roar of 27,000 fans watching history unfold. This is rugby sevens at its most electric — fast, physical, and unforgettable.
RU706.potentialContendersIntro: The gold medal match could feature a rematch of recent World Series finals, with New Zealand the benchmark and the USA a potential home-crowd challenger if both reach the final.

RU707.blurb:
Men's rugby sevens begins with six pool matches across the morning session. Fiji, two-time defending champions, and France, reigning Paris gold medalists, headline a field chasing quarterfinal spots through three days of round-robin play.

Every match is 14 minutes of non-stop rugby — blink and you'll miss a 60-meter breakaway try. Pool seeding determines knockout matchups; finishing top of your group means avoiding the bracket's heavyweights until later rounds.

Carson Stadium transforms into a sevens festival for the men's tournament: drums, costumes, national anthems echoing off the stands. The 27,000-seat bowl has hosted World Rugby Sevens Series stops before — it knows how to throw a rugby party.

Evening pool play continues the men's tournament under lights, with six more matches closing Day 1. Teams that won in the morning look to double up; those that lost face elimination pressure.

Fiji's free-flowing style and France's tactical discipline — shaped by Antoine Dupont's Paris heroics — could both be on display. One upset result can scramble the entire bracket and force favorites into sudden-death knockout matches earlier than planned.

Night sevens at Carson Stadium cranks the energy higher: brighter lights, louder crowds, and the electric buzz of a sport built for spectacle. Every try gets a roar; every tackle gets a gasp.

Fiji and France both aim to finish pool play unbeaten, but sevens punishes complacency — a single lapse in the 14th minute can flip a result. Antoine Dupont, if competing, brings star power and tactical edge; Fiji brings flair and two Olympic golds worth of pedigree.

Carson Stadium fills gradually through the morning, reaching full voice by the session's climax. The compact venue keeps fans close to the action — you can hear the collisions, see the sweat, feel the stakes.

Fiji's attacking brilliance against France's structured game — if both advance — would be a clash of Olympic philosophies. One mistake, one missed tackle, and an Olympic dream ends in 14 minutes.

Evening quarterfinals at Carson Stadium deliver peak sevens intensity: sell-out crowds, knockout stakes, and the sport's signature chaos. The compact venue amplifies every cheer, every groan, every last-second try that reshapes the bracket.

Afternoon sevens at Carson Stadium means blazing sun and maximum energy. Placement matches for fifth through eighth also unfold here — Olympic finishes that carry national pride and future funding back home.

Fourteen minutes to decide Olympic immortality — no timeouts, no second chances, just sevens rugby at its highest stakes. The bronze match beforehand carries weight too: a podium finish at the Olympics is a career-defining result.

Evening finals bring the sevens festival to its peak: 27,000 roaring fans, championship stakes, and the sport's signature speed and spectacle. Carson Stadium's tight bowl design traps the noise and energy, creating an unforgettable cauldron for the final whistle.

SAL01.blurb:
The Pacific becomes a proving ground as kiteboarders launch the LA28 sailing program. Formula kite athletes — foiling above the water at speeds topping 40 knots — open their qualification series off Belmont Shore, the same Long Beach venue that hosted sailing in 1984.

Both men's and women's fields race through late afternoon and early evening conditions, building toward finals on July 19. Wind and wave patterns off the Southern California coast will test different skills than Paris did — adaptability and race-craft matter as much as raw speed.

Belmont Shore offers shore-side viewing close to the action. Watch hydrofoils lift riders clear of the Pacific chop, kites arcing overhead as athletes angle for early momentum in the qualification series.
SAL01.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024 gold medalist Valentin Bontus and multi-time world champion Gisela Pulido are likely to headline their respective fields if they compete, though LA28 rosters remain unannounced.

potentialContenders.name: Valentin Bontus
potentialContenders.note: Won gold in men's Formula Kite at Paris 2024

potentialContenders.name: Gisela Pulido
potentialContenders.note: Multiple-time kiteboarding world champion

SAL02.blurb:
Dual disciplines share the afternoon at Belmont Shore — windsurfers open their qualification series while kiteboarders return for day two. The iQFOiL windsurfing boards make their LA28 debut alongside the continued kite racing, offering a contrast in foiling styles and tactics.

Kite athletes push through their second qualifying day with finals two days away. Windsurfers begin a three-day qualification grind, navigating the coastal breeze patterns that can shift dramatically between afternoon and evening sessions.

The shore provides clear sightlines to both courses. You'll see the kites' vibrant canopies against the sky and the windsurfers' upright stance as they carve turns on hydrofoils — two ways to harness wind and water.
SAL02.potentialContendersIntro: Kite contenders like Paris champion Valentin Bontus and world champion Gisela Pulido could appear alongside yet-to-be-announced windsurfing qualifiers.
