SAL03.blurb:
The qualification picture sharpens. Windsurfers enter day two of their series while kiteboarders race their final qualifying rounds before Wednesday's medal races — pressure mounts as fields narrow toward the finals.

Four disciplines share the water: men's and women's in both kite and windsurfing. The session runs through the prime afternoon wind window into evening, when the Pacific breeze often peaks and foiling speeds climb.

Belmont Shore gives you proximity rarely found in Olympic sailing. The racing happens close enough to shore to see athlete adjustments mid-course — kite angles, board trim, split-second tactical calls as competitors jockey for advancement.

SAL04.blurb:
The first Olympic medals of LA28 sailing are decided. Men's and women's kite finals crown champions in one of the Games' most visually arresting sports — athletes foiling above the Pacific at speeds that blur the line between sailing and flying.

Kite finals unfold as high-speed duels, with lead changes possible on every leg. Meanwhile, windsurfers continue day three of qualification racing, their finals set for Thursday — the session offers both medal drama and the grind of advancement.

Belmont Shore puts you shoreside for the spectacle. Kites cut arcs overhead, boards skim centimeters above the water, and the finish can come down to a single maneuver in the final meters of the course.
SAL04.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024 champions are expected to be in the medal hunt. Valentin Bontus could defend his men's title for Austria, while the women's field awaits confirmation — though Gisela Pulido remains a perennial contender.

potentialContenders.name: Valentin Bontus
potentialContenders.note: Defending Olympic champion, projected to contend for repeat gold

potentialContenders.name: Gisela Pulido
potentialContenders.note: Multi-time world champion, likely contender if she qualifies

SAL05.blurb:
Windsurfing medals close out the board events at Belmont Shore. Men's and women's iQFOiL finals determine Olympic champions in a discipline where athletes stand upright on hydrofoils, controlling board and sail through a blend of balance, power, and tactical precision.

The finalists emerge from three days of qualifying in variable Pacific conditions. Medal races are typically shorter, tighter courses — positions can flip on a single tack, and the podium often isn't settled until the final crossing.

The shore viewing is intimate. You're close enough to see athletes lean into gusts and adjust sail trim mid-flight, foils slicing the water as the finish line approaches and Olympic medals hang in the balance.

SAL06.blurb:
The dinghy racing begins with opening-day qualification rounds for both men (ILCA 7) and women (ILCA 6) fleets. Sailors launch from the Port of Los Angeles into Pacific waters, racing tactical courses where wind shifts and wave reading separate the field.

This is your most accessible ticket into Olympic sailing — six hours of continuous racing as dozens of boats compete in multiple heats. The afternoon Pacific breeze typically builds through the session, rewarding sailors who adapt their strategy.

Spectating from the port gives you sightlines across the race course with downtown LA rising behind the harbor. The opening gun fires, fleets round marks in tight packs, and you watch world-class sailors read conditions in real time.
SAL06.potentialContendersIntro: Top dinghy sailors from Paris 2024 and recent world championships are projected to compete, though LA28 rosters won't be finalized until 2027 qualification events conclude.

SAL07.blurb:
Three classes hit the water — men's and women's dinghies continue qualification while mixed dinghy (470) and mixed multihull (Nacra 17) crews open their campaigns. The Nacras are sailing's Formula 1: twin-hulled catamarans that fly above the water on foils at breathtaking speed.

This session blends second-day dinghy strategy with opening nerves in the two-person boats. Mixed crews race as equals — one sailor traps out over the water while the other steers, both reading wind and coordinating every tack.

The port's industrial backdrop frames a coastal race course where you can watch four different Olympic classes simultaneously. Nacras scream across the water; dinghies weave tactical patterns; the Pacific delivers the stage.
SAL07.potentialContendersIntro: Britain and Australia topped the Paris 2024 sailing medal table and could field strong crews across all three classes if their top sailors qualify for LA28.

SAL08.blurb:
The skiffs arrive. Men's 49er and women's 49erFX crews launch into their opening qualification races — high-performance dinghies with trapeze wires and asymmetric spinnakers that demand athleticism as much as sailing skill. Meanwhile, dinghies hit day three and mixed boats continue building their qualification series.

Four classes racing across a single afternoon makes this the most varied session of the regatta. Skiff crews hike out over the water at radical angles, spinnakers billow in Pacific wind, and tight mark roundings produce spectacular near-collisions.

The harbor hums with activity — support boats tracking every fleet, course marks bobbing in ocean swells, and the LA skyline watching over a sprawling tactical battlefield.
SAL08.potentialContendersIntro: Britain's Dylan Fletcher and Spain's Diego Botín won skiff golds at Paris 2024. If they qualify for LA28, both could return to defend their titles.

SAL09.blurb:
Medal day for dinghy sailors. Men's ILCA 7 and women's ILCA 6 fleets race their finals — single, winner-take-all fleet races where the entire regatta's standings compress into one dramatic showdown. Meanwhile, skiffs hit day two and mixed boats continue qualification.

Dinghy medal races are sailing's purest test: ten boats, one race, points tallied from the entire series with final-race scoring doubled. A sailor in fifth can win gold with a perfect race and a rival's mistake.

The premium ticket price reflects the stakes — you're watching Olympic champions crowned in real time. The Pacific wind doesn't care about pressure; sailors who thrive under it will stand on the podium.
SAL09.potentialContendersIntro: Britain's Michael Beckett and Australia's Matt Wearn won dinghy golds at Paris 2024. If both qualify, a rematch could define the men's final.

SAL10.blurb:
Two more medal races decide Olympic champions. Mixed dinghy (470) and mixed multihull (Nacra 17) crews race their finals while skiffs complete day three of qualification. The Nacra final is sailing's most visually arresting race — catamarans foiling at 30 knots, crews executing high-speed gybes inches above the water.

Mixed boat racing showcases co-ed teamwork at its highest level. In the 470, coordination is everything — trimming sails, calling tactics, weight placement synchronized. In the Nacra, it's raw speed and nerve.

Skiffs continue their buildup toward their own finals, but today the premium seats watch mixed crews fight for medals on a Pacific race course framed by port cranes and the LA coastline.
SAL10.potentialContendersIntro: Italy's Tita/Banti won back-to-back Nacra golds; Australia's Belcher/Ryan took mixed dinghy gold at Paris 2024. Both duos could return if they qualify for LA28.

SAL11.blurb:
The regatta's final act: back-to-back skiff medal races crowning men's 49er and women's 49erFX Olympic champions. These are sailing's most athletic boats — crews hang off trapeze wires, spinnakers twice the size of the hull, everything happening at maximum speed and minimum margin for error.

Skiff finals deliver pure drama. Ten boats, double points, one race to settle a week's worth of qualification. A capsize ends a medal dream; a perfect start can erase a points deficit.

The abbreviated three-hour window keeps the energy relentless. Two races, two gold medals, and the Pacific Ocean as the proving ground for sailing's fastest sailors.
SAL11.potentialContendersIntro: Britain's Fletcher/Bithell and Spain's Botín/Trittel won Paris 2024 skiff golds. Australia's Price/Haseldine took 49erFX silver and could challenge for gold if they return.

SHO01.blurb:
Opening day of Olympic shooting, and the Games' first medals are decided by the smallest margins imaginable. Standing perfectly still at 10 meters, shooters fire at a bullseye the size of a period — hundredths of a point separate podium from heartbreak.

Qualification runs all afternoon as dozens of the world's best riflemen and women chase eight final spots. The evening final is pure tension: each shot projected live, scores updating in real time, one wobble ending Olympic dreams.

Long Beach Target Shooting Hall sits inside the Convention Center, a temporary indoor range steps from the Pacific. Silence between shots, roars when a perfect 10.9 lands — this is precision sport at its most unforgiving.
SHO01.potentialContendersIntro: Tokyo 2020 gold medalist William Shaner could headline the men's field on home soil, while China and South Korea typically dominate women's rifle events.

potentialContenders.name: William Shaner
potentialContenders.note: Tokyo 2020 gold medalist in Men's 10m Air Rifle, competing at home

potentialContenders.note: Tokyo 2020 gold medalist in Women's 10m Air Rifle, projected contender if she qualifies

potentialContenders.note: Paris 2024 finalist, South Korea's likely men's representative

SHO02.blurb:
Air pistol demands extraordinary hand stability — holding a 1.5-pound pistol at arm's length for two hours, controlling breath and heartbeat, finding the center of a target smaller than a dime. China and South Korea have owned this event for decades.

The format mirrors day one: qualification whittles the field to eight finalists, then an elimination shootout under lights. One shaky trigger pull, one inhale at the wrong moment, and four years of preparation evaporate.

The temporary Long Beach range gives spectators an unusual intimacy with shooting — you're close enough to see hands tremble, close enough to feel the hush before each shot breaks.
SHO02.potentialContendersIntro: India's Manu Bhaker won bronze in Women's 10m Air Pistol at Paris 2024 and could return with a billion fans watching. China and South Korea will likely field Tokyo and Paris medalists.

potentialContenders.name: Manu Bhaker

potentialContenders.name: Jin Jong-oh
potentialContenders.note: Multi-Olympic champion, likely contender if he qualifies at age 44

potentialContenders.note: Paris 2024 gold medalist in Women's 10m Air Pistol, expected to compete

potentialContenders.note: Paris 2024 gold medalist in Men's 10m Air Pistol, projected Chinese representative

SHO03.blurb:
The opening day of Olympic skeet qualifying brings shooters from around the world to Whittier Narrows, where clay targets cross at 60 mph and consistency counts for everything. Both men's and women's fields will shoot their first rounds outdoors in the Southern California morning, with top qualifiers advancing to the next day's finals.

Skeet demands lightning reflexes and absolute precision — shooters call for targets at eight stations around a semicircle, and the clays streak overhead from high and low houses. One missed orange disc can be the difference between a medal run and going home.

Whittier Narrows sits tucked into South El Monte's recreation area, an outdoor range where the crack of shotguns echoes across open fields. It's an accessible early-round session with breakfast-hour start times and the chance to watch Olympic hopefuls lay down their opening scores.
SHO03.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024 silver medalist Amber Rutter could return for Great Britain after a controversial final-shot call denied her gold. Expect world-ranked shooters from Italy, the US, and China to compete for qualifying spots.

potentialContenders.name: Amber Rutter
potentialContenders.note: Paris 2024 silver medalist, controversially denied gold by final-shot call

potentialContenders.name: Austen Smith
potentialContenders.note: Paris 2024 bronze medalist, could compete on home soil if she qualifies

potentialContenders.name: Danka Bartekova
potentialContenders.note: Former World Champion, veteran of multiple Olympics

potentialContenders.name: Vincent Hancock
potentialContenders.note: Four-time Olympic gold medalist in men's skeet, projected to compete at home Games

SHO04.blurb:
Mixed team events pair one man and one woman from the same country — a relatively new Olympic format that adds strategy and partnership to individual precision. Air rifle and air pistol teams both compete today, alternating shots in a relay final.

The U.S. could field William Shaner alongside a top women's rifle shooter, giving the home crowd a rooting interest in both disciplines. China, South Korea, and India have all medaled in recent mixed events and will likely bring formidable pairs.

Long Beach's evening session runs both rifle and pistol mixed team finals back-to-back. It's a chance to see shooting's biggest names in a different context — supporting a partner, managing nerves for two.
SHO04.potentialContendersIntro: No LA28 rosters are confirmed, but likely pairings could include Shaner (USA) in rifle and Bhaker (IND) in pistol, based on their recent Olympic success.

potentialContenders.name: William Shaner
potentialContenders.note: Tokyo 2020 gold medalist, projected U.S. mixed rifle representative

potentialContenders.name: Manu Bhaker
potentialContenders.note: Paris 2024 bronze medalist in mixed pistol, expected Indian team member

potentialContenders.name: Park Ha-jun
potentialContenders.note: South Korean rifle shooter, could pair in mixed team if qualified

SHO05.blurb:
Finals day in skeet — both men's and women's champions crowned in a single session. Morning qualifying rounds whittle the field, then the top eight in each event face elimination finals where every miss is magnified.

Under the new ISSF format, eight finalists start from scratch in single-elimination brackets. Targets fly, shooters call, and the field shrinks until two remain for gold. It's ruthlessly efficient and brutally unforgiving — one bad station and you're out.

The afternoon finals take over Whittier Narrows with medal stakes and live crowds watching every shot. Expect cheers when clays shatter and groans when they sail through untouched — skeet finals are decided by fractions of a second.
SHO05.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024 medalists including Amber Rutter and Vincent Hancock are likely contenders if they qualify for LA28. Italy, China, and the United States typically field strong skeet teams.

potentialContenders.name: Amber Rutter
potentialContenders.note: Paris 2024 silver medalist seeking redemption after controversial final

potentialContenders.note: Four-time Olympic champion, projected home-crowd favorite

SHO06.blurb:
Three positions — kneeling, prone, standing — 40 shots each, 120 total. The 50m rifle events are marathons of concentration, two-and-a-half hours where a single technical error costs a medal.

Standing is the killer: no support, muscles screaming, rifle swaying with every heartbeat. The best in the world shoot 10.7s and 10.8s while their bodies beg them to stop. Finals come down to who holds form longest.

Morning light filters into the Long Beach hall as qualification begins. By early afternoon, eight finalists remain for the shootout that crowns the most complete rifle shooter at the Games.
SHO06.potentialContendersIntro: China swept men's rifle medals at Paris 2024 and will likely send reigning champions. Norway and the USA could field strong contenders based on recent world championship results.

potentialContenders.name: Liu Yukun

SHO07.blurb:
The women's 3 Positions final is an exercise in pain management. Kneeling, prone, standing — 40 shots from each, fighting muscle fatigue and mental exhaustion across 120 rounds of precision.

China has dominated women's rifle events for two Olympics running. But standing position is the great equalizer: even the best shooters wobble, and a single wild shot in the final standing series can hand the medal to someone steadier in the moment.

The Long Beach hall hosts this midday grind — morning qualification, afternoon final. You'll watch shooters cycle through positions, scores tightening with every elimination, the gold coming down to who can hold the rifle still when their body is spent.
SHO07.potentialContendersIntro: China's Qiongyue Zhang won gold at Paris 2024 and is the heavy favorite if she qualifies. Switzerland and the USA have fielded recent world-class competitors.

potentialContenders.name: Zhang Qiongyue

SHO08.blurb:
Trap qualification begins with shooters facing clay targets launched at randomized angles from a single bunker. Men's and women's competitors shoot in waves across the morning and early afternoon, each aiming to post scores high enough to reach the final.

Trap is a game of split-second reads — targets can fly left, right, or straight, and shooters have one shell to shatter each orange disc. The best in the world rarely miss, so qualification is often decided by who holds their nerve when the pressure mounts.

Whittier Narrows Clay Center offers open-air shooting against a backdrop of Southern California foothills. It's a chance to see the Olympic field before the medal rounds, with affordable tickets and a laid-back atmosphere before finals intensity arrives.
SHO08.potentialContendersIntro: Nathan Hales of Great Britain won Paris 2024 gold and set an Olympic qualifying record. Expect top-ranked shooters from Italy, China, and the US to compete for spots in the final.

potentialContenders.name: Nathan Hales

SHO09.blurb:
The 25m pistol is split across two days. Today is Precision: slow fire at stationary targets, five seconds per shot, 30 rounds of controlled perfection. Tomorrow comes Rapid Fire — three seconds to get five shots off — but first you have to survive this.

Precision rewards the smoothest technique. Breathe, aim, squeeze — no rushing, no second-guessing. Shooters who can repeat the exact same motion 30 times in a row build the lead that carries them through the chaos of Rapid.

Long Beach's indoor range keeps conditions stable — no wind, no sun glare, just you and the target. It's a midmorning window into the first half of one of shooting's most technical events.
SHO09.potentialContendersIntro: China and South Korea have historically dominated women's 25m pistol. Paris 2024 and recent world championship results will shape the likely LA28 field, though no rosters are confirmed.

potentialContenders.name: Yang Jiin

SHO10.blurb:
Medal day in trap — morning qualifiers complete their rounds, then the top eight men and top eight women advance to finals where Olympic champions are crowned. Two gold medals decided in one packed session.

The new eight-shooter final format raises the stakes. Shooters face elimination rounds until two remain for the gold-medal duel — every target matters, and a single miss can end an Olympic dream. Nathan Hales set an Olympic record en route to Paris gold; expect similar precision here.

Whittier Narrows comes alive for finals, with spectators packed around the range watching clays explode against the California sky. It's outdoor Olympic shooting at its most dramatic — open air, high stakes, and champions made in real time.
SHO10.potentialContendersIntro: Nathan Hales could defend his Paris 2024 gold if he qualifies for Great Britain. Look for Paris medalists Qi Ying and Adriana Ruano Oliva, plus Italian and US shooters in the medal hunt.

SHO11.blurb:
Rapid Fire Stage completes the 25m pistol gauntlet: five shots in three seconds, repeat six times. Yesterday's Precision leaders can blow up here — one trigger snag, one sight picture rushed, and the medal slips away.

The final combines both stages into an elimination shootout. Precision and Rapid scores merge; the steadiest hand and fastest reflexes win. It's the only Olympic pistol event mixing slow and fast fire, rewarding complete shooters.

Long Beach's midday session builds from Rapid Fire's frenetic morning into the afternoon final. The crowd hears the staccato crack of five quick shots, then dead silence as scores post — pure shooting theater.
SHO11.potentialContendersIntro: South Korea's Yang Jiin won Paris 2024 gold and is the projected favorite. France and Hungary claimed Paris silver and bronze and could return if they qualify.

SHO12.blurb:
Mixed team trap brings pairs of shooters together — one man, one woman per nation — competing for a single Olympic medal. It's trap's newest format, and the single-elimination finals create instant drama.

Teams qualify in the morning, then the top four pairs face knockout brackets in the afternoon. Partners alternate shots, and one missed target puts pressure on the other to deliver. It's individual precision meeting team stakes, with gold decided in a winner-take-all final.

Whittier Narrows wraps up its shotgun program with this mixed event, where national pride meets partnership shooting. The outdoor range setting and team format make it a distinctly different vibe from individual trap — collaborative, high-energy, and wide open for upsets.
SHO12.potentialContendersIntro: No mixed team trap rosters are confirmed for LA28 as of April 2026. Expect pairings from Italy, China, Great Britain, and the US based on individual trap strength and Paris 2024 results.

SHO13.blurb:
Rapid Fire Pistol is shooting's most explosive event: five targets, five shots, all in four seconds. Later rounds drop to three seconds, then two. It's reflex and rhythm, the pistol blurring across targets while the brain barely keeps up.

Stage 1 is the qualifier — shooters fire 60 rounds across two series, chasing eight final spots. The best in the world make it look easy, the pistol snapping left-to-right in perfect time. One jam, one misfire, and you're watching the final from the stands.

Long Beach's late-afternoon session is the opening act. The crowd hears the rapid-fire crack echoing through the hall, sees scores flash, and starts learning which shooters have the speed to medal tomorrow.
SHO13.potentialContendersIntro: China's Li Yuehong won Paris 2024 gold in Men's 25m Rapid Fire Pistol and is the likely favorite if he returns. Germany and France have recent world-class competitors.

SHO14.blurb:
Stage 2 completes qualification, then the eight fastest shooters face off in the final. Four seconds becomes three, then two — at that speed, conscious thought is too slow. Muscle memory takes over, and medals go to whoever drilled the pattern deepest.

China dominated this event at Paris 2024, sweeping gold and bronze. South Korea claimed silver. If those shooters return, the final will be a showcase of the world's fastest precision — five shots, two seconds, repeated until one shooter stands alone.

It's the last rifle and pistol session of the Games, a fitting closer to 11 days of shooting. Long Beach's evening final delivers rapid-fire drama and the final medals decided by fractions of a second.
SHO14.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024 champion Li Yuehong of China is the projected favorite if he qualifies. South Korea's Cho Yeongjae and China's Wang Xinjie could also return based on their Paris medals.

SKB01.blurb:
The women's street preliminary opens Olympic skateboarding in LA, where 20 skaters will battle through runs and tricks to advance eight finalists. This is your accessible entry into the sport at its permanent Olympic home — prelims offer the best ticket value and a chance to see the full global field before cuts narrow the bracket.

The format blends technical precision with creative risk: two 45-second runs down rails and stairs, followed by five single-trick attempts where scores can spike or crash in seconds. Paris gold medalist Coco Yoshizawa could return at 18, now a veteran; watch for rising talents pushing boundaries to crack the final.

Sepulveda Basin in the San Fernando Valley hosts the competition, though Venice Beach Skatepark — the sport's spiritual birthplace — looms in promotional imagery. The venue atmosphere skews younger and louder than most Olympic events, with DJ soundtracks and a crowd that knows every technical name for every trick.
SKB01.potentialContendersIntro: Coco Yoshizawa won Paris at 14; if she competes in LA at 18, she'd be the headline returner. Expect a mix of established names from Paris and younger qualifiers breaking through.

SKB02.blurb:
Nyjah Huston could skate his home Olympics here — the Paris gold medalist is from the LA area, and no venue carries more symbolic weight for street skating than Southern California. The men's prelim seeds 20 skaters into an eight-man final through two runs and five trick attempts.

Yuto Horigome, the Tokyo gold and Paris bronze medalist, represents Japan's technical school; if both he and Huston advance, their final could define the event. The format rewards both consistency in runs and explosive single-trick gambles — watch for skaters saving their hardest tricks for the final attempts when elimination looms.

The session runs midday at Sepulveda Basin, a valley venue far from the beach but close to skateboarding's SoCal DNA. Expect a crowd fluent in the sport's language, vocally appreciating cleanly landed tricks and groaning at slams.
SKB02.potentialContendersIntro: Nyjah Huston won Paris and could compete at home; Yuto Horigome is the reigning Tokyo champion. The field will likely include top qualifiers from recent Street League and world championship circuits.

SKB03.blurb:
Eight women, two runs, five tricks, gold on the line. The street final is skateboarding's highest-stakes 90 minutes — skaters who survived the morning prelim now chase medals under evening lights with zero margin for error.

If Coco Yoshizawa qualifies, she'd defend her Paris title at 18 in front of a SoCal crowd that knows every technical detail. The format allows dramatic comebacks: a single landed trick in the final attempts can vault a skater from fifth to gold. Expect calculated aggression — conservative runs to bank a score, then high-risk tricks when medals demand it.

Sepulveda Basin's temporary stands will pack tight for this session, the sport's signature Olympic moment. Skateboarding crowds are participatory and loud, reacting in real time to every attempt, every slam, every make.
SKB03.potentialContendersIntro: Coco Yoshizawa is the Paris champion and projected favorite if she competes. Japan swept the Paris podium; expect their technical skaters to dominate qualifying into this final.

SKB04.blurb:
The Men's Street Final brings the world's best technical skateboarders to a purpose-built urban course at Valley Complex 1. Gold is decided in two phases: runs judged on consistency and flow, then a best trick showdown where single moments of brilliance can change everything.

Nyjah Huston, the reigning Paris champion, is the heavy favorite if he competes — this is his home turf, his sport, his legacy on the line. Yuto Horigome, the Tokyo gold medalist who took bronze in Paris, remains the most technical rider on the planet, capable of landing tricks no one else attempts.

The session runs late evening under lights, with a Southern California skate crowd that knows every rail and every stair set. When someone lands a career-defining trick in the best trick phase, you'll hear it echo across the valley.
SKB04.potentialContendersIntro: Paris medalists and world championship contenders are projected to fill the final field. Huston and Horigome headline, but the street skating landscape is deep and unpredictable.

SKB05.blurb:
Twenty skaters drop into the bowl for their first runs of the Games, hunting one of eight final spots. Defending two-time Olympic champion Keegan Palmer of Australia faces his opening test, while American Tom Schaar gets a home crowd advantage in his qualifying heat.

Park prelims are best-of-three — each skater gets three 45-second runs, and only the highest score counts. Expect conservative early runs as athletes feel out the course, then progressively bigger tricks as they chase qualification.

Valley Complex 2 sits north of downtown, a temporary park course built for the Games. The stands wrap the bowl close enough to hear boards clatter on coping.
SKB05.potentialContendersIntro: Keegan Palmer won back-to-back golds in Tokyo and Paris and enters as the heavy favorite. Tom Schaar, who finished 6th in Paris, could gain momentum skating for the home nation.

potentialContenders.name: Keegan Palmer
potentialContenders.note: Defending Olympic champion, won gold at Tokyo 2020 and Paris 2024

potentialContenders.name: Tom Schaar
potentialContenders.note: 6th place Paris 2024, competing at home as top American contender

SKB06.blurb:
The women's park field is famously young — at Paris, the podium averaged under 16 years old. This prelim session introduces the next wave of teenage prodigies alongside returning medalists from France.

Paris gold medalist Arisa Trew of Australia, now 16, could appear if she continues competing. Sky Brown of Great Britain, a Tokyo and Paris bronze medalist, would be among the oldest in the field at 20. Each skater gets three runs to post their best score for a top-eight final berth.

Evening session under Valley Complex lights, the course a canvas for aerial creativity. Park skating rewards style as much as difficulty — watch for skaters who make impossible tricks look casual.
SKB06.potentialContendersIntro: Arisa Trew won Paris gold at just 14 and could return as a favorite if she qualifies. Sky Brown, a two-time Olympic bronze medalist, would bring veteran presence to a field dominated by teenagers.

potentialContenders.name: Arisa Trew
potentialContenders.note: Paris 2024 gold medalist at age 14, known for landing tricks no one else attempts

potentialContenders.name: Sky Brown
potentialContenders.note: Bronze medalist at Tokyo 2020 and Paris 2024

SKB07.blurb:
Eight finalists, three runs each, one Olympic champion crowned. Keegan Palmer could attempt an unprecedented third consecutive park gold, but the format rewards risk — one flawless 45-second run beats two safe ones.

Scoring blends technical difficulty with flow and amplitude. Judges watch for clean landings, varied tricks across the course, and the height skaters achieve launching from the deep end. A single fall zeroes a run, so finals become a high-wire act of ambition versus execution.

Prime-time session at Valley Complex 2, the temporary park course roaring with a crowd that understands the difference between a backside 540 and a McTwist. Medals decided in 75 minutes of controlled chaos.
SKB07.potentialContendersIntro: Keegan Palmer of Australia seeks a historic third straight Olympic gold. Tom Schaar of the USA, skating in front of a home crowd, could challenge if he elevates from his 6th-place Paris finish.

potentialContenders.name: Keegan Palmer
potentialContenders.note: Two-time defending Olympic champion (Tokyo 2020, Paris 2024)

potentialContenders.name: Tom Schaar
potentialContenders.note: 6th place Paris 2024, projected as top American medal threat

SKB08.blurb:
The women's park final showcases skateboarding's youth movement — Tokyo's podium averaged 14 years old, Paris went even younger. Medals will likely go to skaters born after 2008, with trick progressions accelerating faster than any previous generation.

Arisa Trew of Australia could defend her Paris gold if she qualifies, while Sky Brown of Great Britain might chase her third consecutive Olympic medal at 20. Best-of-three scoring means conservative runs rarely win — expect commitments to tricks that have never been landed in competition.

Evening session under Valley Complex lights, the final event of the park competition. The temporary course gets torn down after this, so skaters treat it like a blank canvas with nothing to lose.
SKB08.potentialContendersIntro: Arisa Trew could defend her Paris gold if she competes for Australia. Sky Brown of Great Britain, a two-time Olympic medalist, would bring championship experience to a likely field of teenagers.

potentialContenders.name: Arisa Trew
potentialContenders.note: Reigning Olympic champion, won Paris 2024 gold at age 14

potentialContenders.name: Sky Brown
potentialContenders.note: Two-time Olympic bronze medalist (Tokyo 2020, Paris 2024)

SQU01.blurb:
Squash's Olympic debut opens with the first-ever Olympic round of 16 matches under glass at Universal Studios. Eight men's and women's matches will begin narrowing a 32-athlete field toward history's first Olympic squash medals.

Egypt is projected to send multiple athletes in both draws based on world rankings, while the USA's home quota ensures American representation. The all-glass Comcast Squash Center lets you watch the action from every angle, with sightlines unmatched in traditional squash venues.

Evening primetime start means you'll see the inaugural Olympic squash matches under lights with a Hollywood backdrop. The glass court setup transforms a typically intimate sport into a spectator showcase.

SQU02.blurb:
Sunday night continues squash's opening round with four more Round of 16 matches under the Universal Studios lights. The 16-athlete draws in both men's and women's singles mean every match carries quarterfinal stakes.

Qualification pathways through Continental Games and world rankings could bring together champions from five continents. Egypt's depth in both draws sets up potential intra-country battles before the medal rounds.

The temporary glass court venue offers what traditional squash clubs can't: stadium seating with unobstructed views from all sides. You'll hear every shot echo off the glass walls.

SQU03.blurb:
The third night of Round of 16 action brings squash's Olympic debut deeper into the bracket. With only 16 athletes per gender competing, these Monday matches carry the weight of do-or-die elimination.

Qualification through five Continental Games across 2026-2027 means this draw will represent every corner of the squash world. The PSA world rankings route ensures the sport's top talent, while universality spots could introduce breakthrough stories from emerging nations.

The glass court setup at Universal Studios turns a traditionally enclosed sport into theater. You'll watch from wraparound seating as athletes battle in a transparent box with nowhere to hide.

SQU04.blurb:
Tuesday night closes the Round of 16 with the final four matches before quarterfinals. Every point in these glass-court battles determines who advances to medal contention in squash's first Olympic tournament.

The qualification system spreads spots across Continental Games winners, world rankings, and host nation quotas, creating a field balanced between established powers and geographic diversity. Up to three countries per gender can send two athletes, potentially setting up elimination clashes between teammates.

Watch from stadium seating as the glass walls amplify every rally. The transparent court makes you feel closer to the action than any traditional squash club could offer.

SQU05.blurb:
Quarterfinals night brings squash's Olympic debut into the final eight. Four matches across men's and women's singles will determine who advances to within one win of a medal.

Egypt could dominate both draws if top-ranked athletes qualify as projected through world rankings and the African Games. The maximum-three-NOCs rule for two-athlete quotas means potential all-Egyptian quarterfinals in either gender.

The glass court under primetime lights creates a pressure cooker. You'll see every grimace, every celebration, every shot ricocheting off transparent walls just feet from your seat.

SQU06.blurb:
Thursday's quarterfinals complete the final eight with four more do-or-die matches. Winners advance to semifinals and guaranteed medal matches; losers go home from squash's first Olympic tournament.

The draw could feature Continental Games champions from Asia, Africa, Europe, the Americas, and the Pacific facing off against world-rankings qualifiers. Egypt's projected depth means potential teammate-versus-teammate elimination battles.

Glass walls at Universal Studios turn squash from a club sport into amphitheater spectacle. The all-angle sightlines let you track every angle, every retrieval, every drop shot that would be invisible in traditional venues.

SQU07.blurb:
Women's semifinals put two Olympic medals within reach. The final four will battle for spots in Sunday's gold medal match, with semifinal losers guaranteed bronze-medal contests.

Egypt has produced dominant women's players in recent years, with Nouran Gohar holding the world No. 1 ranking entering the qualification period and Nour El Sherbini a multiple-time world champion. If both qualify and advance, an all-Egyptian semifinal could determine the gold medal favorite. Amanda Sobhy could give the USA its first Olympic squash medal on home soil.

Friday night under glass at Universal Studios. Two matches, four athletes, history-making stakes in a sport that waited decades for this moment.

SQU08.blurb:
Men's semifinals determine who plays for squash's first-ever Olympic gold medal. Two matches, four survivors from a 16-man draw, all chasing a podium spot in the sport's long-awaited Olympic debut.

Ali Farag, a former world champion, and Mohamed ElShorbagy, a multiple-time former world No. 1 now competing for England, could meet in a semifinal if both qualify and advance. Egypt's depth in men's squash means the African Games quota and world rankings routes could send multiple Egyptian and English stars into this round.

Saturday night at Universal Studios. Glass court, Hollywood backdrop, the final four under primetime lights with medals on the line.

SQU09.blurb:
Squash's first Olympic women's medals will be awarded tonight. The bronze match settles third place; the gold medal match crowns the sport's inaugural Olympic champion after decades of campaigning for inclusion.

Nouran Gohar entered the qualification period as women's world No. 1, while Nour El Sherbini is a former champion with sustained dominance. If both qualify and reach the final, it could be an all-Egyptian gold medal match. Amanda Sobhy carries American hopes for a home-soil breakthrough.

Sunday night finale at Universal Studios. The glass court, the medals ceremony, history written in real time as squash finally joins the Olympic program.

SQU10.blurb:
The first Olympic men's squash gold medal will be decided Monday night. Bronze match followed by the gold medal final, closing out a sport's long-awaited Olympic debut with its first-ever champion.

Ali Farag, a former world champion, and Mohamed ElShorbagy, a multiple-time former world No. 1 now representing England, are projected contenders if they qualify and advance through the bracket. Egypt's squash dominance could produce the sport's first Olympic champion.

Monday night at Universal Studios. Glass court, medals podium, the culmination of squash's Olympic journey under the Hollywood lights.

SRF01.blurb:
Surfing comes home. The Olympics return to California waters at Trestles, one of the most revered waves in competitive surfing — a high-performance point break that's hosted World Surf League finals for decades.

Round 1 runs both men's and women's fields through opening heats. The format is unforgiving: lose here and you're into an elimination round. Expect to see defending champions and local favorites paddling out early, setting the tone for the entire competition.

You'll watch from the bluffs above Lower Trestles, close enough to see spray fly off the lip. The 7 AM start catches the morning glass — offshore winds grooming long, rippable walls on both lefts and rights.
SRF01.potentialContendersIntro: The field will include recent world champions, Paris medallists, and California natives surfing in front of a home crowd — exact rosters aren't final, but expect the sport's elite.

potentialContenders.note: Defending Olympic champion from Paris 2024, trains in California and knows Trestles well

SRF02.blurb:
The elimination round. Surfers who dropped their Round 1 heats get one more chance; winners advance, losers go home.

Trestles delivers consistent, long-walled waves powered by Southern Hemisphere swells — unlike Tahiti's raw power, this is precision surfing. East-northeast winds groom the face into a canvas for aerials, carves, and high-speed rail work. Round 2 is where strategies sharpen and the field tightens.

Morning sessions catch clean conditions before the wind shifts. You're watching world-class athletes surf one of the world's best waves with their Olympic dreams on the line.
SRF02.potentialContendersIntro: Round 1 losers fight to stay alive — likely a mix of former world champions, Paris medalists, and emerging contenders who stumbled in the opening heat.

SRF03.blurb:
The final prelim before the medal rounds. Round 3 narrows the field to quarterfinalists — only eight men and eight women will surf tomorrow for medals.

Lowers breaks best on southwest swells with northeast offshore winds; July conditions often deliver. The wave offers multiple sections — some surfers will hunt the inside for technical combos, others will sit deep and wait for set waves. Every ride counts.

The bluffs fill with fans who know what they're watching. Trestles has been a pilgrimage site for California surfers for generations — this is the wave where careers are made.

SRF04.blurb:
Medal day at Trestles. Quarterfinals, semifinals, and gold medal matches — men's and women's — all in one session.

This is what surfing's Olympic moment was building toward: the best surfers on earth on one of the best waves on earth. Caroline Marks could defend her Paris gold on California waves. Gabriel Medina could reprise his aerial wizardry. A local like Caitlin Simmers could claim gold on her home break. The stakes are absolute.

The session runs all day, tide and swell dependent. Bluff seating puts you close enough to hear boards crack the lip. Trestles has hosted World Surf League crownings for decades — today it crowns Olympic champions.

SWA01.blurb:
The duet technical routine is artistic swimming's most demanding discipline — precision choreography, impossible lifts, and synchronized artistry, all while holding your breath. Eighteen duets will compete in this preliminary round, with the top performers advancing to the free routine final the next day.

China leads the world rankings after dominating the 2025 World Cup, while Spain and Japan have emerged as consistent podium threats. The USA, swimming at home with a guaranteed host-nation quota, could field strong contenders drawn from their Paris 2024 medal-winning team.

Long Beach Aquatics Center sits right on the Pacific, with ocean breezes and natural light turning the pool into an open-air theater. You'll see the technical precision that separates elite duets from the rest — every extension, every breath timed to the millisecond.
SWA01.potentialContendersIntro: China is projected to lead the field based on their 2025 World Cup victory, with Spain and Japan likely to challenge after recent podium finishes.

SWA02.blurb:
Two swimmers, one four-minute routine, and a medal on the line. The duet free final is artistic swimming's creative showcase — less rigid than the technical, with space for theatrical flourishes and risk-taking choreography that can wow judges or fall flat.

China's Wang twins won gold in Paris and the nation sits atop the world rankings heading toward LA28. Spain and Japan have posted consistent podium finishes recently, while Team USA could ride home-crowd energy after medaling in Paris 2024.

The Long Beach venue puts you close to the action — close enough to see the strain on faces during underwater holds, close enough to hear the crowd gasp at a perfectly executed lift. Medals are decided here, and the California sun will be blazing overhead.
SWA02.potentialContendersIntro: China's Wang twins won Paris 2024 gold and could return, though no LA28 rosters are confirmed. Spain, Japan, and the USA are projected to challenge based on recent form.

SWA03.blurb:
Eight swimmers moving as one organism — the team technical routine is a test of synchronization so perfect it seems inhuman. This preliminary session winnows the field before the acrobatic final, with teams executing mandatory elements under strict judging.

China swept team golds at the 2025 World Championships and won the Paris 2024 team event by 82 points. The USA took silver in Paris, their first team medal since 2004, and will have the California crowd behind them as hosts. Spain claimed Paris bronze.

Long Beach Aquatics Center's outdoor setting means you'll watch under open sky, with the Pacific visible beyond the deck. The technical routine is all business — no room for improvisation, just flawless execution of elements that look physically impossible.

SWA04.blurb:
The team free routine is where artistic swimming gets theatrical — four minutes of choreography that blends athleticism with performance art. Teams build narrative arcs, take creative risks, and chase scores that can separate gold from fourth by fractions of a point.

China controlled the Paris 2024 prelims and went on to win gold by a dominant margin in the final. Team USA earned silver, ending a 20-year medal drought, while Spain took bronze. All three are expected to return, though rosters won't be finalized until qualification concludes in 2028.

This is your chance to see the free routine before the final — teams testing choreography, gauging the judges, feeling out the competition. Long Beach's coastal venue and California crowd could turn this prelim into something louder and more charged than a typical early round.

SWA05.blurb:
The acrobatic final is artistic swimming's biggest spectacle — eight swimmers launching each other skyward in lifts and throws that defy gravity. LA28 introduces a mixed-team format allowing up to two male athletes per squad, fundamentally changing the power and height of the routines.

China won Paris 2024 team gold by 82 points and swept the 2025 World Championships team events. The USA took silver in Paris, their first team medal in two decades, and will have home advantage in Pasadena. Spain claimed Paris bronze, though no nation has confirmed its LA28 roster yet.

Long Beach Aquatics Center becomes a sunlit arena for this final — medals decided, national anthems played, the Pacific Ocean stretching beyond the deck. The acrobatic routine is the sport's signature moment, and the California crowd will be at full volume.

SWM01.blurb:
Opening morning of Olympic swimming brings the first heats at the brand-new Rose Bowl Aquatics Center. Seven events scatter across the pool — butterfly, breaststroke, backstroke, distance free, and both relay teams — each one surfacing the qualifiers who'll race for medals tonight and beyond.

Torri Huske, the Paris 100m fly champion, will likely be in the water for the women's butterfly heats. David Popovici, Romania's Paris 400m free gold medalist, could appear in the men's distance event if he's racing that distance in LA. The Americans are projected to field deep squads in both 4x100m free relays.

Prelims are the accessible entry point — lower prices, a chance to see the full global field before cuts narrow the bracket. The venue is outdoor, every seat close to the water, Pasadena morning light filtering into the bowl.
SWM01.potentialContendersIntro: Paris medalists and world-ranked sprinters are projected to appear across this multi-event morning session, particularly in the 100m fly and 400m free heats.

SWM02.blurb:
First finals session under the Pasadena sky. The men's 400m free and women's 400m free crown their champions, the 4x100m free relays deliver opening-night fireworks, and four semifinal heats set the stage for tomorrow's sprints.

Popovici could defend his Paris 400m free title if he races the event in LA. The American women's and men's relay teams are likely medal contenders based on their Paris form. Finals are where the atmosphere peaks — night racing, tight finishes, the outdoor bowl amplifying every roar.

Every seat is close to the water at the Rose Bowl Aquatics Center. You'll feel the splash off the starting blocks.

SWM03.blurb:
Sunday morning heats span breaststroke, backstroke, the 100m free, and Léon Marchand's signature 400m IM. The French star won four individual golds in Paris; if he's racing the IM in LA, this is where he begins his title defense.

Mollie O'Callaghan took silver in the Paris 100m free; she could appear in these heats. The men's 100m back and women's 100m breast are wide-open sprint events with deep international fields. Morning sessions offer a chance to see lanes full of qualifiers before the semifinal cuts.

The Rose Bowl Aquatics Center sits in a canyon adjacent to the stadium. Morning sun, close sightlines, and the hum of a global field warming up.

Olympic mixed doubles is famously unpredictable — gold has scattered across nations and pairings in recent Games. Paris 2024 saw China's Zheng Qinwen and Zhang Zhizhen claim gold; LA28 will write a new chapter.

TKW01.blurb:
Opening day of Olympic taekwondo features the lightest weight classes — women's -49kg and men's -58kg — as 64 athletes across two divisions begin their medal runs. The rounds of 32 and 16 compress rapid-fire elimination matches into four hours, where a single head kick can end an Olympic dream.

South Korea dominated both divisions at Paris 2024, with Kim Yu-jin and Park Tae-joon claiming gold. If defending champions return, they'll face challengers from Iran, Turkey, and emerging programs eager to upset Korea's decades-long taekwondo supremacy.

LA Convention Center Hall 1 puts you close enough to hear every spinning kick connect. The intimate setup captures the intensity of each three-round bout, with head kicks echoing through the hall and crowds reacting to every point scored on the electronic body protectors.

If either returns and reaches the final, they'll face the pressure of defending Olympic titles on home soil in front of a Convention Center crowd packed with Korean-American fans and taekwondo practitioners from LA's thriving dojangs.

Evening session under bright competition lights creates the sport's signature atmosphere — electronic scoring pads lighting up with every clean strike, instant replays on big screens showing whether a spinning head kick landed flush. TRI01.blurb:
The women's individual triathlon kicks off at sunrise on Venice Beach — a 1.5km ocean swim, 40km bike along the coast, and 10km run back to the finish. Paris gold medalist Cassandre Beaugrand could return to defend her title, though the field is deep with contenders from Great Britain, the U.S., and Bermuda.

This is draft-legal racing: athletes can slip behind competitors on the bike to save energy, then unleash their run speed in the final 10km. Expect pack tactics on the bike and a blistering footrace to the line.

Venice Beach at dawn is a spectacle — palms silhouetted against the sunrise, the Pacific surf churning, and thousands lining the boardwalk as athletes emerge dripping from the ocean. You'll see the full course unfold in real time.

TRI02.blurb:
Alex Yee won Paris gold by running down the leader in the final kilometer — a finish so dramatic it became instant Olympic lore. If he returns, expect another tactical masterclass: conservative swim, patient bike, explosive run.

The men's race is a 1.5km swim, 40km bike, 10km run gauntlet decided in the closing kilometers. Hayden Wilde's blazing run speed makes him a projected threat, and the field could include France's Léo Bergère and rising Americans.

Venice Beach offers unmatched sightlines — you'll watch the swim start from the sand, track the bike peloton as it loops past lifeguard towers, and feel the crowd roar as runners sprint down the boardwalk. Early start means cool ocean air and golden light off the Pacific.

TRI03.blurb:
Four athletes, two per gender, each racing a mini-triathlon then tagging the next teammate — the mixed relay is chaos distilled. Teams draft aggressively on the bike, sprint all-out on the run, and hand off in a designated exchange zone.

France, Great Britain, and the U.S. are projected powerhouses if they field their stars. The relay format rewards versatility: one weak leg can sink a medal bid, but a breakaway swim or blistering run can steal victory.

The Venice Beach course compresses the action — you'll see multiple athletes racing simultaneously, the crowd erupting with every exchange, and lead changes on every lap. It's triathlon's most electric format, and the palm-lined finish straight will be pandemonium.

Mixed doubles moves fast — quarterfinals loom tomorrow. China could field multiple pairs, Japan's pairings are always live, and upsets happen when the format compresses.

TTE14.blurb:
Another singles Round of 32 session — the bracket is sharpening, and every match is a potential quarterfinal preview. Top seeds like Fan Zhendong, Chen Meng, and Sun Yingsha are expected to cruise if they qualify, but the Round of 32 is where underdogs make their Olympic legacy.

This is pure singles focus — no doubles to share the stage. Expect tight games, long rallies, and the occasional five-set war.

Hall 3 in the afternoon is table tennis for the price of a nice dinner. The sightlines are tight, the crowd is knowledgeable, and the sport is faster than it looks on TV.

TTE15.blurb:
Two women's doubles medals on the line — bronze followed by gold. Chen Meng and her Paris partner won gold; Sun Yingsha could appear in a different pairing if she qualifies for LA28. China's depth makes this event a near-lock for multiple podium spots.

The bronze is a fight to salvage the week. The gold is tactical perfection — every serve matters, every return is calculated.

Hall 3 under the lights is where table tennis feels like theater. The crowd picks sides, the acoustics amplify every rally, and the medals hang in the balance.

TTE16.blurb:
The Round of 16 opens with both men's and women's singles in morning action at the LA Convention Center. Across multiple tables, you'll see the field narrow from 32 to 16 in each draw — the last chance for dark horses to upset the Chinese juggernauts before the knockout rounds tighten.

The format is best-of-seven to 11 points, and upsets happen fast. One cold streak, one mistimed serve, and a favorite is out.

Hall 3 is a working convention space turned Olympic arena — the roar off the hard walls makes every point echo. Watching multiple matches unfold simultaneously gives you the sport's full speed and variety in one session.
TTE16.potentialContendersIntro: Top seeds from Paris 2024 are projected to advance comfortably, but the Round of 16 is where rising challengers make their names.

TTE17.blurb:
The afternoon Round of 16 session continues the cull in both singles draws. By the end of this block, half the field is gone — sixteen survivors move on to Friday's quarterfinals.

The LA28 program expands table tennis to include a new Mixed Team event, but singles remain the sport's glamour showcase. This is where the world watches China's depth get tested.

The ITTF president has called Hall 3's 7,000-seat capacity too small for the sport's growing popularity. Expect a packed, loud room — table tennis crowds know when to erupt.
TTE17.potentialContendersIntro: The same Paris 2024 contenders are likely to dominate, with Japan and European challengers hunting for upsets.

TTE18.blurb:
The final Round of 16 session plays out under primetime lights. This is the last knockout before the quarterfinals — winners punch their ticket to medal contention, losers head home.

Hall 3 served as the Main Press Headquarters in the 1984 LA Olympics; now it's a stripped-down table tennis battleground. The evening crowd brings extra energy, and the acoustics turn every smash into a cannon shot.

By 10 p.m., the quarterfinal field is set. If you want to catch rising stars before they're household names, this is the session.
TTE18.potentialContendersIntro: No LA28 rosters are confirmed yet, but Paris 2024 medalists and world-ranked contenders are projected to fill the draw.

TTE19.blurb:
The quarterfinals arrive. Eight men, eight women, all one match from a guaranteed medal.

The stakes shift here — every point matters, and the Chinese delegation's depth gets its sternest test. In Paris 2024, Fan Zhendong and Chen Meng took gold; their rivals are hunting revenge.

Olympic singles matches are best-of-seven to 11 points, which means momentum swings fast. Hall 3's acoustics amplify every rally, every roar. You're watching the sport at its highest level, where reflexes are superhuman and margins are razor-thin.
TTE19.potentialContendersIntro: The quarterfinals are projected to feature Paris 2024 medalists and top-ranked challengers, though LA28 qualification is still ahead.

TTE20.blurb:
The afternoon quarterfinal session continues the medal hunt. Winners are guaranteed bronze at minimum; losers go home empty-handed.

Fan Zhendong remains world number one in men's singles entering 2026. If he and Wang Chuqin both reach the final again, their rivalry could define LA28 table tennis the way it has defined the sport for years.

This is your chance to see the world's best in a high-stakes, high-speed format. Hall 3's converted convention-floor atmosphere keeps it intimate — every facial expression, every frustrated swipe of the paddle, is visible from the stands.
TTE20.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024 champions and top-ranked contenders are expected, with Japan and European nations hunting for breakthrough medals.

TTE21.blurb:
The final quarterfinal session plays out Friday night. By 11 p.m., the semifinal fields are locked — four men and four women, all guaranteed medals.

In Paris 2024, China swept the men's and women's podiums. Breaking that stranglehold is the holy grail for every other nation, and the quarterfinals are where it starts.

Hall 3's hard walls turn every rally into a thunderclap. The late-night energy adds edge to an already nerve-shredding format. If you want to witness the moment a non-Chinese player breaks through, this is where it happens — or doesn't.
TTE21.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024 medalists and world-ranked contenders are projected to fill the quarterfinal draw, with no official LA28 rosters yet announced.

TTE22.blurb:
The women's singles semifinals. Two matches, four players, all fighting for a place in Saturday night's gold medal match.

Chen Meng won gold in Paris 2024; Sun Yingsha took silver. If both reach LA28 and meet again, it's China's breathtaking depth on full display — a reminder that the country's women's program is as dominant as any dynasty in Olympic sport.

Hall 3's intimacy makes the semifinals feel electric. You're close enough to see the spin on the ball, the sweat on the brow. This is where legends are made or broken.
TTE22.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024 medalists Chen Meng and Sun Yingsha are projected to compete if they qualify, alongside top-ranked challengers from Japan and Europe.

TTE23.blurb:
The women's singles gold and bronze medal matches close out Saturday night. This is the sport's showcase — the fastest hands in the world, playing for Olympic immortality.

China has won nearly every women's singles gold since 1988. Chen Meng is a two-time champion; Sun Yingsha is the heir apparent. If they meet in the final again, it's a masterclass in precision and power.

Hall 2 is the showcase court for medal matches, with acoustics that amplify every rally. The crowd roars, the ball blurs, and someone walks away an Olympic champion. Table tennis doesn't get bigger than this.
TTE23.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024 gold and silver medalists Chen Meng and Sun Yingsha are projected finalists if they qualify, with challengers from Japan and Europe hunting for upsets.

TTE24.blurb:
The men's singles semifinals. Two matches, four players, one step from the gold medal match.

Fan Zhendong won Paris 2024; Wang Chuqin is his greatest rival. Their meetings define modern table tennis — lightning-fast rallies, unreadable serves, inhuman reflexes. If both make the final, it's the marquee matchup of LA28 table tennis.

This is your chance to see the sport at its absolute peak. Hall 3's converted convention space keeps it intimate — every facial tic, every paddle adjustment, is part of the drama.
TTE24.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024 gold medalist Fan Zhendong and rival Wang Chuqin are projected to compete if they qualify, with Japan's Tomokazu Harimoto a leading non-Chinese contender.

TTE25.blurb:
The men's singles gold and bronze medal matches under Sunday night lights. This is the holy grail — breaking China's stranglehold on Olympic table tennis.

Fan Zhendong won Paris 2024; Wang Chuqin took silver. If they meet again in the final, it's a rivalry match for the ages. If an outsider breaks through — Japan's Harimoto, a European dark horse — it's one of the biggest upsets in Olympic history.

Hall 3's 7,000-seat capacity is too small, according to the ITTF president, which means tickets are scarce and the atmosphere is molten. The acoustics turn every point into a roar. You're watching history.
TTE25.potentialContendersIntro: Paris 2024 finalists Fan Zhendong and Wang Chuqin are projected to compete if they qualify, with Japan and Europe hunting for a breakthrough.

TTE26.blurb:
The Mixed Team event debuts at LA28 — a historic first for Olympic table tennis. Groups 1 and 2 open Round 1 on Monday morning, with nations fielding pairs of men and women in a team format.

China's depth is staggering. If Fan Zhendong and Chen Meng pair up, or Wang Chuqin and Sun Yingsha, it's a juggernaut. Japan, led by Harimoto, and European nations will test their mettle in the group stage.

Hall 3's intimacy and hard-wall acoustics make team matches feel like a street fight. The energy is different from singles — teammates on the bench, national pride on the line. You're witnessing Olympic history being written.
TTE26.potentialContendersIntro: No LA28 rosters are confirmed yet, but China is projected to dominate with pairs from its Paris 2024 champions. Japan and European nations are likely contenders.

TTE27.blurb:
Groups 3 and 4 take the floor Monday evening for Mixed Team Round 1. The format is new, the stakes are Olympic, and every nation is figuring out the chemistry on the fly.

China's dominance in singles doesn't guarantee team success — cohesion matters, and upsets happen when one partner has an off night. This is where dark horses make noise.

The evening session at Hall 3 brings a louder, looser crowd. Multiple matches run simultaneously across tables, giving you the full scope of the event. If you want to catch the sport's newest frontier, this is it.

TTE28.blurb:
Mixed Team Round 2 continues Tuesday morning. Groups 1 and 2 return, with standings tightening and knockout brackets taking shape.

China's Paris 2024 champions — Fan Zhendong, Wang Chuqin, Chen Meng, Sun Yingsha — are projected to anchor the Chinese squad if they qualify. Japan, led by Harimoto, and European challengers are hunting for upsets in the group stage.

Hall 3's stripped-down convention-floor setup keeps the atmosphere raw. The ITTF president has complained the venue is too small for the sport's popularity, which means packed stands and deafening rallies. You're watching a brand-new Olympic event find its identity in real time.
TTE28.potentialContendersIntro: No LA28 rosters are confirmed, but China is projected to dominate with pairs from its Paris 2024 champions. Japan and Europe are likely challengers.

TTE29.blurb:
Groups 3 and 4 return for Mixed Team Round 2 on Tuesday night. The group stage is nearing its end; teams are jockeying for knockout positioning.

China's depth in both men's and women's singles could translate to Mixed Team dominance, but the format is new enough that no one knows for sure. Japan's Harimoto and China's Fan, Wang, Chen, and Sun are all active on the WTT circuit as of 2025-26, with no reported injuries.

Hall 3's evening sessions have extra bite — the crowd is looser, the stakes are rising, and the acoustics turn every smash into a stadium event. This is the Olympic debut of Mixed Team table tennis, and you're here for it.
TTE29.potentialContendersIntro: No LA28 rosters are confirmed, but China and Japan are projected to be the top contenders based on Paris 2024 results and current world rankings.

TTE30.blurb:
Mixed Team Round 3 closes out the group stage Wednesday morning. Groups 1 and 2 play their final matches before the knockout brackets are set.

This is the last chance for underdogs to force upsets and reshape the bracket. China is projected to cruise, but the Mixed Team format is new — one bad match, one unexpected pairing, and the whole thing flips.

Hall 3 served as the Main Press Headquarters in 1984; now it's an Olympic table tennis battleground. The room holds around 7,000, which the ITTF president has called too small. Expect a full house and a roaring crowd. You're watching the Olympic debut of a new event, and the stakes are just beginning to rise.
TTE30.potentialContendersIntro: No LA28 rosters are confirmed, but China and Japan are projected to lead Groups 1 and 2 based on Paris 2024 results and world rankings.

TTE31.blurb:
The mixed team event makes its Olympic debut at LA28, and this session catches two groups in their final round-robin matches. Teams blend their best men's and women's players into one squad — expect China to field some combination of their stacked roster, while Japan, South Korea, and European nations scrap for quarterfinal spots.

The format is fast and unforgiving: best-of-seven games, each to 11 points, with the roar of 7,000 fans echoing off the convention hall walls. Group stage intensity ramps up here as teams fight to avoid early elimination.

LA Convention Center Hall 3 is a converted multi-use space — industrial bones dressed up for Olympic competition. The atmosphere is intimate and loud, every smash reverberating through the stands.
TTE31.potentialContendersIntro: China is projected to dominate both groups, likely fielding combinations of their Paris 2024 gold medalists. Japan and European teams could push for upset wins.

Opening morning of beach volleyball on California sand — a quintessential LA moment. Three preliminary matches kick off the 24-team tournaments for men and women, setting the tone for two weeks of ocean-view competition.

Teams face their first Olympic test in best-of-three sets, with pool seeding on the line. Expect a mix of reigning champions and rising contenders fighting for early momentum.

Alamitos Beach Stadium sits right on the Long Beach waterfront with Pacific views and morning sun. The crowd is laid-back early, building energy as the day unfolds.

VVO01.blurb:
The Games open with men's volleyball prelims at Honda Center in Anaheim, a loud 17,000-seat arena that will pack in the crowds for this morning tipoff. With 12 teams vying for knockout spots, every match matters — pool standings determine quarterfinal seeding.

France arrives as the defending Paris 2024 gold medalist, Poland as silver medalist, and the USA as host nation with a guaranteed berth and home-court advantage. Expect powerhouse programs from Europe, South America, and Asia battling for position.

Honda Center's intimate sightlines put you close to the court — you'll feel every thunderous spike and see the strategy unfold in real time.
VVO01.potentialContendersIntro: France won gold in Paris; Poland took silver. The USA is guaranteed a spot as host and enters ranked No. 3 in the world. Exact rosters won't be set until 2026–2028 qualification concludes.

potentialContenders.name: France
potentialContenders.note: Defending Paris 2024 Olympic gold medalists

potentialContenders.name: Poland
potentialContenders.note: Paris 2024 silver medalists, perennial European power

potentialContenders.name: USA
potentialContenders.note: Ranked No. 3 in the world, won bronze at Paris 2024

potentialContenders.name: Brazil
potentialContenders.note: Historically dominant program, likely to qualify

VVO02.blurb:
Afternoon prelims continue at Honda Center as the pool stage heats up. Teams are fighting for position — finish top-two in your pool and you control your quarterfinal matchup.

The USA men enter as the No. 3 ranked team in the world and Paris 2024 bronze medalists, with recent VNL Finals results (6-1) showing strong form heading into LA28. A home crowd could turn Honda Center into a fortress.

Sit mid-bowl for the best view of the entire court — you'll see setters orchestrate attacks and defenders dig impossible shots off the hardwood.
VVO02.potentialContendersIntro: The USA won bronze in Paris and is ranked No. 3 globally. If the U.S. plays in this session, expect stars from their current VNL roster to appear.

VVO03.blurb:
Evening prelims bring prime-time energy to Honda Center. By now, pool standings are taking shape — teams know what they need to advance, and desperation can produce heroic volleyball.

France won Paris 2024 gold; Poland took silver. The USA has gone 6-1 in recent Finals and 4-1 at Worlds, projecting themselves as a medal threat on home soil. Qualification runs through 2026–2028, so final rosters remain fluid.

The 5 PM slot catches the arena filling up — casual fans mingling with diehards, the buzz of a crowd settling in for a long night of volleyball.
VVO03.potentialContendersIntro: France and Poland are the reigning Olympic finalists. The USA is ranked No. 3 and won Paris bronze. Expect these three if they draw this time slot.

Late-night volleyball at Honda Center — the final prelim session of the day brings a rowdy crowd and high stakes. Teams that stumbled earlier need a statement win; contenders want to lock up their pool.

France and Poland are the Paris 2024 finalists; both are projected to qualify and could meet here if the draw falls that way. The USA, as host, gets an automatic berth and enters with home-court swagger.

A 9 PM start means the arena is at full throat — this is when volleyball gets loud, chaotic, and unforgettable.
VVO04.potentialContendersIntro: France won gold in Paris; Poland took silver. The USA is guaranteed a spot. Rosters won't finalize until 2028, but these three are near-locks to be here.

Sunday morning prelims kick off another day of pool play at Honda Center. With qualification running through 2028, no rosters are final — but France (Paris gold) and Poland (Paris silver) are both strong bets to return.

The USA has a guaranteed host spot and recent form (6-1 in Finals, 4-1 at Worlds) that suggests they'll be competitive. Early risers get affordable tickets and a chance to see rising stars before the knockout rounds.

Morning sessions have a different vibe — quieter, more tactical, fans settling in with coffee as teams probe for weaknesses.

VVO06.blurb:
Afternoon prelims continue as the pool stage reaches its midpoint. The USA men, ranked No. 3 in the world and Paris 2024 bronze medalists, have medaled four times in VNL history — they know how to peak for big tournaments.

With a 19-11 overall record in 2025 and dominance at NORCECA Championships (gold or silver every year since 1981), the Americans are a safe bet to contend on home soil. Honda Center's crowd will be heavily partisan.

Sit lower bowl for the full sensory experience — you'll hear the squeak of shoes, the thud of spikes, the roar after every point.
VVO06.potentialContendersIntro: The USA is ranked No. 3 globally and won Paris bronze. Long Beach State's 2025 NCAA title shows the depth of American volleyball. Expect USA stars if they're scheduled here.

VVO07.blurb:
Women's volleyball arrives at Honda Center, and the atmosphere shifts — the women's game is faster, more tactical, and arguably deeper than it's ever been. Italy won a dramatic Paris 2024 gold; the USA finished 5th after a quarterfinal loss to Brazil.

Both teams are projected to return: Italy ranked No. 1 in the world, the USA No. 2. The Americans beat Italy 3-1 in the 2025 VNL final week, suggesting they've closed the gap. A home crowd could be the edge they need.

Evening sessions bring energy — fans arrive after work, the arena hums with anticipation, and every serve feels consequential.
VVO07.potentialContendersIntro: Italy is the Paris 2024 gold medalist and No. 1 in the world. The USA is No. 2 and won the recent VNL showdown 3-1. Both are expected to compete at LA28.

The USA finished 5th in Paris but is a perennial medal contender and will lean into home-court advantage. With rosters still being finalized through 2028, this session could feature either powerhouse or an emerging challenger.

A 9 PM start brings the weekend crowd — louder, looser, ready for drama. Honda Center's acoustics amplify every cheer.

VVO31.blurb:
The women's volleyball tournament opens with the first of six prelim sessions spread across the weekend at Honda Center in Anaheim. Twelve teams will battle through pool play to reach the quarterfinals, with the USA guaranteed a spot as host nation and the defending Paris 2024 champion Italy expected to headline the field.

This afternoon session showcases two of the four opening matches as teams debut lineups and test their Olympic conditioning. Prelims are the most accessible entry point to Olympic volleyball — ticket prices bottom out here, and you'll see the full roster depth as coaches rotate lineups across the pool schedule.

Honda Center's 17,000-seat bowl will be loud even for a Saturday afternoon, and the Anaheim arena brings volleyball back to Orange County for the first time since the 1984 Games. Expect drums, flags, and national anthems echoing off the rafters as the tournament begins.

The two-lift format — snatch, then clean & jerk — rewards both finesse and guts. A missed lift can end a medal dream; a gutsy third attempt can rewrite the standings in seconds.

Peacock Theater's 7,100 seats put you close enough to hear chalk dust and grunts, close enough to feel the platform shake when 350 pounds hit the floor.

WLF03.blurb:
Two weight classes, one marathon session — the 61kg and 69kg women's finals unfold back-to-back over nearly five hours. Mid-weight categories produce the sport's most balanced athletes, strong enough to move serious weight yet agile enough for balletic technique.

The 61kg lifters open the session with precision lifting. The 69kg class follows with heavier attempts and tighter competition, where a single successful jerk can swing gold.

Peacock Theater becomes a crucible as the afternoon stretches into evening. You'll witness two complete competitions, each its own story of calculated risk and raw power.

WLF04.blurb:
The 75kg men walk the line between middleweight speed and heavyweight power. These athletes move big weight with an explosiveness that makes 400-pound lifts look effortless — until you see the bar bend under load.

Three attempts in the snatch, three in the clean & jerk. Strategy matters: go conservative and leave medals on the platform, or gamble on a final lift that could deliver glory or heartbreak.

Peacock Theater's 11 PM finish makes this a primetime event under lights. The crowd's proximity turns every successful lift into a shared eruption, every miss into collective silence.

WLF05.blurb:
Two heavyweight classes collide in a single session — the 85kg and 95kg men, where brute strength meets athletic grace. The 85kg lifters move with the precision of middleweights; the 95kg athletes announce that power has arrived.

Each class crowns its own champion across six lifts. The contrast is stark: watch a lean 85kg lifter dance under a bar, then see a 95kg giant muscle it overhead through sheer force.

Peacock Theater hosts nearly five hours of heavy metal hitting the platform. The ascending weight attempts build to a crescendo as lifters chase podium positions with final-round gambles.

WLF06.blurb:
The 77kg women occupy the sweet spot of Olympic weightlifting — heavy enough for jaw-dropping totals, athletic enough for technical brilliance. These lifters routinely snatch over 130kg and clean & jerk north of 160kg, weights that would humble most gym-goers.

The final unfolds as a tactical chess match. Lifters and coaches calculate attempts in real time, weighing safe bronze against risky gold with every kilogram added to the bar.

Peacock Theater's late-night energy suits this session. You're close enough to see the mental battle before each lift, the ritual chalk-up and deep breath before stepping onto the platform.

WLF07.blurb:
The 86kg women are among the sport's most powerful athletes, launching weights that test the limits of human strength. This category sits between light heavyweight and super heavyweight — lifters built for force but still possessing the speed to snatch 140kg overhead in a blink.

Six attempts separate Olympic champion from also-ran. A successful snatch sets the tone; a dominant clean & jerk seals the deal. Miss twice at the same weight and the competition moves on without you.

Peacock Theater's Friday evening slot draws a crowd ready to witness the spectacle. When a 160kg clean & jerk hits the platform, you'll feel it in your chest.

WLF08.blurb:
One weight class below the super heavyweights, the 110kg men deliver a perfect blend of mass and mobility. These are big humans moving big weight with surprising speed — snatches over 180kg, clean & jerks approaching 230kg.

The format is unforgiving: three chances to nail the snatch, three more in the clean & jerk, with the combined total deciding medals. A failed lift can't be repeated; strategy becomes a high-stakes gamble.

Peacock Theater's intimate setting magnifies every moment. Late Friday night, under lights, you'll watch giants attempt the nearly impossible — and occasionally succeed.

WLF09.blurb:
The super heavyweights — no upper weight limit, just the strongest humans on the planet. Lifters in the +110kg class routinely snatch over 210kg and clean & jerk north of 260kg, totals that redefine what's physically possible.

This is the marquee men's event, the one that crowns Olympic weightlifting's undisputed king. Georgia's Lasha Talakhadze won three consecutive golds through Paris 2024; if he competes in LA, he'll chase a historic fourth.

Peacock Theater on Saturday evening becomes a temple of strength. When 500+ pounds crash to the platform after a successful lift, the sound is seismic. You'll leave convinced you've seen something superhuman.

The final event of the weightlifting program crowns the strongest woman in the world. The +86kg class has no upper weight limit — just lifters built for absolute power, snatching 150kg and clean & jerking 180kg like it's a warmup.

China's Li Wenwen dominated Paris 2024 with a 297kg total in the +81kg category; if she competes at +86kg in LA, she's the one to beat. Canada's Maude Charron, a consistent podium threat, could challenge.

Peacock Theater's Saturday night finale delivers maximum drama. The crowd leans in for every lift, knowing they're witnessing the culmination of four years of training compressed into six decisive attempts.
